Implicit vs. explicit declaration of what is and what is not part of the CLI interface is a constant battle (ex: see similar ongoing battle between using $PATH or absolute paths to executables).

For all the inconvenience of the implicit approach (well described above) using @cmdline decorators has the advantage of creating a single point of change in the code base. When you have a big team and manage a big code base, "magic" actually trumps anything else. It is well worth the occasional headache.

As per RFC 3986, all URL data should be UTF-8 encoded before being
percent-encoded. That is, percent-encoded URL components should be bytewise
encoded from UTF-8 code points.

From Section 2.5, Identifying Data:

> When a new URI scheme defines a component that represents textual data
> consisting of characters from the Universal Character Set [UCS], the data
> should first be encoded as octets according to the UTF-8 character encoding
> [STD63]; then only those octets that do not correspond to characters in the
> unreserved set should be percent- encoded.

  * https://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc3986.txt

As such, there's not a strong case for furl to deviate from this behavior, nor
allow an option to deviate from this behavior and potentially confused users.


That said, thanks to your example, I now understand that you want to
percent-encode non-UTF8 encoded strings, a la `u'/你好吗'.encode('gb2312')`. But
I'm still a tad confused why you'd like to do that. The resultant
percent-encoded URL data, e.g. '/%C4%E3%BA%C3%C2%F0' from `quote(u'/你好
吗'.encode('gb2312'))` will be decoded incorrectly by the software that consumes
that URL, which will expect valid UTF8 code points but instead find gb2312 code
points. Like:

```python
>>> from urllib import quote, unquote
>>> quote(u'你好吗'.encode('gb2312'))
'%C4%E3%BA%C3%C2%F0'
>>> unquote('%C4%E3%BA%C3%C2%F0').decode('utf8')
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/encodings/utf_8.py", line 16, in decode
    return codecs.utf_8_decode(input, errors, True)
UnicodeDecodeError: 'utf8' codec can't decode byte 0xc4 in position 0: invalid continuation byte
```

So, please help me understand why you want to percent-encode non-UTF8 encoded
strings (like gb2312 encoded strings) into the final URL, non-comformant to RFC
3986? That will help determine how furl can help solve your problem.

With 0.2.0 release I changed the way stdout/stderr is displayed and returned. The `run` method now has two new arguments: `stdout` and `stderr` which specify stdout and stderr redirection respectively. By default they are both set to `None` which means no redirection: everything is printed into the running terminal. You can also use `subprocess.PIPE` as the value for `stdout` to get stdout as the return value. I think this is what you want. Check out the doc [here](http://ffmpy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ffmpy.html) and the last example [here](http://ffmpy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/examples.html) for more explanation.

Ok, imagine, we have 5000 tasks already scheduled to run. And we have 10000 connections to poll.
If we push them to the ready queue all at once, we end up with 15000 tasks. This might snowball, so we might end up with a pretty fat ready queue, like millions of tasks. It'll take a while for event loop to clean up all those tasks before it is available to do a next cycle. Sure, the loop will keep going, maybe even without any performance degradation, but we might eventually run out of memory or we will just have to wait for some time before the loop will react to other tasks.

I think it's more common to find projects sticking with 79 chars than the other way around, as that's what PEP-8 dictates. As a consequence 79 is the default in many linters such as `pep8` and `flake8` which are used by most editors and IDEs in order to provide real time syntax-highlighting of uncompliant lines. For instance, Sublime assumes 79 by default and probably also PyCharm and Eclipse.

```
>>> ureg = pint.UnitRegistry()
>>> q = 1 * (ureg.farad * ureg.ohm)
>>> q
<Quantity(1, 'farad * ohm')>
>>> q.to_base_units()
<Quantity(1.0, 'second')>
```
or you can use `q.to(unit)` to convert to any other unit of your choice

Hi, I ran into the same problem today (2 years after the issue was reported). I
solved it by monkeypatching the original code at 3 places

Basically, it involves modifying function __load_types() at 3 locations:
ethernet.py
ip.py
ppp.py

You need to replace 

        d = dict.fromkeys([ x[:-3] for x in os.listdir(os.path.dirname(__file__) or
'.') if x.endswith('.py') ])

with:

    try:
        d = dict.fromkeys([ x[:-3] for x in os.listdir(os.path.dirname(__file__) or
'.') if x.endswith('.py') ])
    except OSError:
        \# py2exe compatibility
        tmp = __import__('dpkt')
        if hasattr(tmp, '__loader__'):
            zipfiles = tmp.__loader__._files.keys()
            d = dict.fromkeys([x.split(os.sep)[-1][:-4] for x in zipfiles if 'dpkt'
in x])

Hope it helps someone. The solution isn't elegant, but works for me.

  Jirka

We should move from optparse to argparse, and move as much logic as possible from bin/pyang into the library.  If you have a need for this ticket to be fixed, you probably know what needs to be done ;-)

Here's some notes I wrote earlier:

An application will have to do something like:

    pyang.init_plugins(plugindirs)
    [<optionally create an optparse object> 

    pyang.add_options(optparse)]
    repos = pyang.FileRepository(path, no_path_recurse)
    ctx = pyang.Context(repos)


Currently, each plugin adds its own options to the optparse object, but if it called from within an application there won't be an optparse object.  This might be ok, but then the plugins rely on the optparse.Value object to check what to do.  Maybe it is ok if the application creates such an object itself, sets the option values it needs to set, and then continue?

Or we could provide a pyang method that would create a suitable optparse.Value instance, with the default values pyang expects, for the application to retrieve and modify as desired.

This would be backwards incompatible, and all plugins would have to be adjusted.

Also, time to introduce a plugin API version, so that we can give a proper error message if an old plugin is being used with a newer version of pyang.

Hi, thanks for reporting.

Parallelization in python is based on the serialization of the objects (using [pickle](https://docs.python.org/2/library/pickle.html#module-pickle)).

Your NotWorking class does not have custom [__setstate__](https://docs.python.org/2/library/pickle.html#object.__setstate__) and [__getstate__](https://docs.python.org/2/library/pickle.html#object.__getstate__) methods and therefore it uses the methods from the parent np.ndarray class, that do not save your custom `self.lookup` dictionary.

Here is the implementation of your parent class for you to have an idea:
 - [np.ndarray.__setstate__](http://docs.danse.us/DrChops/current/ndarray/ndarray/html/NumpyNdArray_8py-source.html#l00327)
 -  [np.ndarray.__getstate__](http://docs.danse.us/DrChops/current/ndarray/ndarray/html/NumpyNdArray_8py-source.html#l00327) 

This shows how pickle does not serialize `lookup`:

```python
import numpy as np

        return obj

def assertAttributesPresent(data):

    assert hasattr(data, 'lookup'), "Lookup dict not present"

That's how it works.  It's scanning an image pyramid. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pyramid_(image_processing)

Think about it. It's scanning a fixed sized HOG detector over the pyramid.  So if you have objects in your images that are smaller than the HOG detector then it will never find them, unless you upsample the image so that the pyramid will include them at a scale >= the HOG detector size.

The issue you've raised (thank you!) is not that obvious how to resolve...

The trade off I can see is this: if we leak user (and thus pretty much all) exceptions up to the caller, then when pysnmp wants to communicate its own errors upwards it will need to piggyback user exceptions which looks quite fragile because the structure of user exception object can be anything.

The other way round when user exceptions are piggybacked on pysnmp exception objects seems more reliable and predictable, though not that convenient and obvious to the user. This is what #168 is trying to implement.

The best way would probably be to use [PEP3134](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-3134/), but that does not work with aged Pythons...

```python
>>> set([0.0, 1e-100])

I am currently using the following monkey patch until `StrictVersion` in schedulers is replaced with `pkg_resources.parse_version`, the expected supported manner (See https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/issues/1310#issuecomment-377078459)

This is due to StrictVersion throwing a ValueError, a rather unique exception to see on a package import. As is usual one must run the patches before any prior import.

```
def patch_redbeat():
    ''' Fix https://github.com/sibson/redbeat/issues/88
    '''
    try:
        from redbeat import schedulers
    except ValueError:
        from distutils import version
        cls = version.StrictVersion
        assert isinstance(cls, (type, old_style_class))
        version.StrictVersion = parse_version
        from redbeat import schedulers
        schedulers.StrictVersion = parse_version
        version.StrictVersion = cls
    else:
        if hasattr(schedulers, 'StrictVersion'):
            assert isinstance(schedulers.StrictVersion, type(parse_version))
```

Currently, git-repos has `hub|bit|lab create` than takes one parameter - name. Using git config's files vs some other file is great - for all the reasons provided and then some. Where does not matter as much as being in human readable file that is managed by git. There would be more parameters in the git-repo config but it would not be a dramatic change and would still be stateless.

A simplistic hypothetical:
- uses the default namespace to configure both gitlab and github repos with: squash commits: protected master, public viability, and description. 
- configures the gitlab repo to mirror github. 
- github is not the development space, so its branching, issues and wiki are disabled.
```
[gitrepo "default"]
   allow_push_master = none
   merge_style = squash

[gitrepo "myprecious"]
    type = gitlab

    mirror_push = github

[gitrepo "github"]
    token = YourOtherVerySecretKey
    allow_push_branches = none
    allow_issues = none
    allow_wiki = none
```

Found the problem. The "typing" events we use are different from key presses, and meant for alternative inputs like speech or handwriting. It allows programs to feed pieces of text to other programs, almost but not quite as if it was typed by a keyboard.

I believed those events were self-contained and stateless, but apparently they count as "key presses", and require a "key release". Just like you can type "ab" by holding "a" and pressing "b", `keyboard` was sending many "key press" events and no "key release", which kinda works but doesn't allow you to type repeated keys.

The solution was to duplicate all typing events, pairing them up with key releases.

If the problem persists, or if you find some related issue, feel free to comment here and I'll reopen the issue.

This is not a use case pycparser was specifically designed for. In general, compilation to AST loses syntactic information. But if you don't care about the emitted code looking as the original, that may be not very problematic. Also note that pycparser doesn't preprocess but expects preprocessed code, which will have all macros expanded, etc. So the code will look quite differently from the original code.

Moreover, when you use fake headers to parse types, these types are lost. The fake headers `typedef` all types to just `int`. If you want to avoid using fake headers, parsing your project becomes much more challenging if it depends on system headers that have a bunch of compiler-specific stuff pycparser doesn't support.

While I'm not saying it's impossible for small contained problems, I would expect using pycparser for this purpose would be quite challenging for "real life" C code bases. I would suggest using Clang's tooling library for this purpose -- https://clang.llvm.org/docs/LibTooling.html

The issue is that the netlink protocol doesn't allow the STP state management. At least in kernels prior to 4.x, but I believe that in 4.x as well. So we have to use the sysfs to do so. The sysfs code for bridges and bonds is integrated and is working, but is a bit tricky to access.

As a proof-of-concept I made a shortcut in the `IPLinkRequest` class:
```
from pyroute2 import IPRoute
from pyroute2 import IPLinkRequest

ip = IPRoute()
idx = ip.link_lookup(ifname='br0')[0]
ip.link('set', **IPLinkRequest({'index': idx,
                                'kind': 'bridge',
                                'stp_state': 1}))
ip.close()
```

The `IPLinkRequest` is a helper class that allows to simplify the NLA construction, which can be very complicated in some cases. You can play around and print `IPLinkRequest` objects for different cases to see, what is the result. It's not my caprice, it's the NLA structure that should be passed to the kernel.

Notes:
1. the `kind` reference is required to correctly handle the `stp_state`.
2. the `IPLinkRequest` should be passed with `**`, as a keyword args dictionary.

…

The `NLMSG_ERROR` response is normal — the kernel returns it for some requests. Most important that `error` is `None`. If it is not `None`, the library throws an exception, so you will not miss an error.

The goal of this script is to make getting https as easy as possible for people in their default environments. @dpkp Earlier we were just comparing current-offset (record.offset) with the desired offset (fetch_offset) once and return immediately after setting desired offset to current-offset+1 inside 'take' api (https://github.com/dpkp/kafka-python/blob/master/kafka/consumer/fetcher.py#L943)

Note: As part of my testing I observed that this issue was only seen with topics with 'COMPRESSED' messages. On testing with UNCOMPRESSED messages, I observed that the reason it wasn't seen was that the current-offset (message.offset) was either equal to fetch_offset or 1 less than the fetch_offset.
 
Let's pick the scenario where max_records is 1 ==> part_records will always have 1 record.
Let's say current-offset (message: m): 80, Desired-offset: 90
Before fix:
----------
The message 'm' is skipped since (80 < 90) and we return, len(part_records) == 1, which is incorrect since we don't havn't reached the record with offset 90.

As a result in next call to _append returns the message with offset 81.

With Fix:
--------
I keep in while loop unless I get max_records (1) message(s). As a result, it iterates from message with current-offset 80 upto the message with current-offset (90: desired-offset). Also, I return the length of relevant messages found.

For docs, my thinking is that to start we can have pydicom.github.io (I'm building a github pages for it) that will link to each of the docs for pydicom and pynetdicom3, respectively. For this solution, you can simply migrate the current to point to the new location (which likely needs a bit of renaming wherever you own the domain) and we will link to it. For a long term solution, I wanted to suggest that I'll start to migrate the current pydicom docs to have it's own part of the site at pydicom.github.io.

Yes - it is as simple as the button! And feel free to change the ownernship and permissions on the repo to give yourself only write access, etc. If I maintain write for any reason I give my assurance that I will never touch any part of the code directly - always via standard PR, review, and then merge by you.

Do you mean continuous integration testing for the containers?  If that is the question then yes, for all containers / functions, etc that I plan on writing, there should be tests to run to check each PR, and any new addition must come with a proper test. Or do you mean a test for someone to run locally before they start developing? Or a test (at the onset of starting some provider with a setting) to make sure that if a user selects a particular kind of reading/decompression, the libraries are there to support that? My approach would be to set them up to do deployment and development in a container that already has (and has been tested to work with) the dependencies. 

I'll still futzing around a bit with figuring out some of the basics, but I'll definitely be able to start a robust doc base soon. To give you some idea of the direction I'm going - we are migrating a bunch of old PACS from many places to Google Cloud, but after some pipelines for de-identification. This means that we need different dicom services, both for provider and user, that can be spun up on demand, and connect both to more "old school" systems, but then also feed into more modern databases (think object based storage for images and datastore (nosql) for metadata). Then for both of those, we will have basic user interfaces to search/query, etc. However, at the end of the day, all of those interfaces will still need to feed back into being accessible via the Dicom protocol, which is why having this base of classes is so important. As for the reason to bring together the open source community and bring people together (generally) around python /dicom, the reason is because we have need to build a lot of tools that can plug into these data sources (for machine learning, or even basic search to integrate with other data sources) and I've found that python is readily digestible by the machine learning / data scientist cohort at Stanford, and it works beautifully with things like Google Cloud APIs.

Anyway, the way I see it, lots of exciting things to think about, design, and build! I'm really glad to have you join, and look forward to helping out with pydicom :) If there is an idea/test/just about anything that you want us to start developing, feel free to create a project to keep a shared list. Or create an issue on a board. And also definitely point your colleagues to the organization and invite those who are interested!

I *think* you'd need to:
- figure out how big the incoming image to that layer is, let's say its 7x7, so then you'd need (I think):

100c7z-tanh
```
I'm not entirely sure, not having ever tried it.  It might be that my analysis above ('above' meaning, 'in this thread') is not quite correct.  Let's try it and see what happens.

For your particular example, if the incoming image is 3x96x96, and your netdef is:
```
netdef=16c3z-tanh-mp2-32c3z-tanh-mp2-64c3z-tanh-mp2-128c3z-tanh-mp2-100n-tanh-2
```
Then, the conv layers are zeropadded, so dont change the image size (as long as kernels have an odd size, which they do, ie 3). And tanh doesnt change image size either.  So we just need to look at the max poolings:
```
input: 96
mp2: 96 => 48
mp2: 48 => 24
mp2: 24 => 12
mp2: 12 => 6
```
So seems that the image size is 6, at the point it enterrs the `100n` layer, so I *think* it'd become:
```
100c6z-tanh
```

Hey!

You don't really get the dataframe until you call the ``.to_pandas()`` method... 

The way **Dask** works is by storing pointers to the data and not processing it until you call the ``.compute()`` method.

Here's an example:

```python

item = collection.item('AAPL')

# convert the entire item to pandas:
df = item.to_pandas()

# item.data = holds the dask dataframe
# item.metadata = holds the metadata

# to get only part of the data as a Pandas dataframe, use:
df = item.data.loc['2017-01-01':'2017-12-31'].compute()

# you can also filter by other methods:
df = item.data[item.data['close'] > item.data['open']].compute()

# you can also delegate calculations to Dask: 
item.data['sma'] = item.data['close'].rolling(200).mean()
df = item.data.compute()
```

I hope that helps :)

I highly recommend you learn more about [Dask's dataframes](http://dask.pydata.org/en/latest/dataframe.html).

You can pass any option to mlaunch, and if mlaunch doesn't know it, it will check mongod's or mongos's --help text. If it's present there, it will pass it on to that process. You also might want to use --oplogSize if you're worried about disk space: 

So `mlaunch init --replicaset --sharded 3 --smallfiles --oplogSize 300` should work. 

- This feature isn't mentioned in the help text, there's a request to change this: #356 
- It is mentioned in the [mtools wiki](https://github.com/rueckstiess/mtools/wiki/mlaunch)
- See also related open feature request #188

Hello,

We've been experiencing this issue for about half a year. We finally got things working with the upgrade to SQLAlchemy 1.1 and using UPSERTS with postgres. Then I overrode the put operation in the SQLAlchemyStore by creating a derived class.

```
class StgoSQLAlchemyStore(SQLAlchemyStore):
    def __init__(self, bind, metadata, tablename):
        SQLAlchemyStore.__init__(self, bind, metadata, tablename)

    def _put(self, key, data):
        con = self.bind.connect()
        with con.begin():
            # delete the old
            con.execute(self.table.delete(self.table.c.key == key))

            insert_stmt = insert(self.table).values(
                key=key,
                value=data
            )

            upsert_stmt = insert_stmt.on_conflict_do_update(
                index_elements=['key'],
                set_=dict(value=data)
            )
            
            # upsert new
            con.execute(upsert_stmt)

            # commit happens here

        con.close()
        return key
```

Finally, inject this store into the init of kvsession

```
session_store = StgoSQLAlchemyStore(engine, db.Model.metadata, 'kvsession')
kvsession.init_app(app=app, session_kvstore=session_store)
```

#33 is fine. I like that it uses the secure algorithm by default. PyBitmessage contains a copy of pyelliptic so that it isn't broken by these sorts of changes. I could manually make the changes that are present in #35 and take them out after an upgrade period. My upgrade plan would be for PyBitmessage to verify the signature using both SHA1 and SHA256 and wait for everyone to upgrade. After a while, clients could start generating signatures using SHA256 and then the custom code could be taken out so that it matches pyelliptic. This is what I did for issue #17. I'll just have to be sure that alternative Bitmessage implementations are prepared. 

While we're on it, you might like to switch to EVP_DigestInit_ex. 
Per https://www.openssl.org/docs/crypto/EVP_DigestInit.html : 
"The functions EVP_DigestInit(), EVP_DigestFinal() and EVP_MD_CTX_copy() are obsolete but are retained to maintain compatibility with existing code. New applications should use EVP_DigestInit_ex(), EVP_DigestFinal_ex() and EVP_MD_CTX_copy_ex() because they can efficiently reuse a digest context instead of initializing and cleaning it up on each call and allow non default implementations of digests to be specified.
EVP_DigestInit() behaves in the same way as EVP_DigestInit_ex() except the passed context ctx does not have to be initialized, and it always uses the default digest implementation."

md_ctx is already being initialized with EVP_MD_CTX_init so it appears to me that EVP_DigestInit could be switched to EVP_DigestInit_ex if you want.

Thinking about it, the code pretty much falls into two regimes:

1) Image-plane - here the data is well structured and we have prior knowledge of how the best store data. For example, we know how many image pixels are in a mask, how my sub pixels are on the image pixel grid and how many deflection angles / light intensities we'll need to compute. This lends itself straight forwardly to NumPy arrays, which can be optimized via Cython.

2) Source plane - here, all hell breaks loose. Our source plane matrices are sparse (f, F, H), and due to the random nature of the traced image coordinates and clustering we have no knowledge of how a given matrix should be set up in 1D. Thus, we have opted for a dictionary representation, which does not rely on predefined array sizes so as to exploit sparsity.

Linking the image-plane NumPy arrays to the source plane dictionaries is straightforward. My concern is the dictionary representation (which handles the most intensive routines) may prove difficult to optimize, as it cannot be directly optimized via Cython.  Sure, it completely circumvents sparseness issues, but it may just be slower in pure processing terms than NumPy arrays with Cython

Is there an efficient way to represent f, F and H using NumPy arrays which is amenable to Cython, Or do these arrays require copious amounts of size tracking / indexing to run?

If we used 2D numpy arrays for f, F and H, can we efficiently track where the non-zero terms are to overcome sparseness? Certainly, we have what we need here for f and H already, and our contiguous source-plane search for F might provide what we need there too. 

(For now, we'll get the code up with NumPy in image plane, dicts in source-plane, then back to Numpy for the inversion, and profile it. Just tracking what the best optimization strategy might be - albeit its hopeless without profiling!)

To hide a column you need to use the `set_column` options not a format. See the [`set_column`docs](https://xlsxwriter.readthedocs.org/worksheet.html#worksheet-set_column).

Here is a fix based on your example:

```python
import xlsxwriter as xlsw

workbook = xlsw.Workbook('hello.xlsx')
worksheet = workbook.add_worksheet()

worksheet.write('A1', 'Hello world')

worksheet.set_column('A:A', None, None, {'hidden': True})

workbook.close()

```

The project relies on many part of the standard library but most of it can be expected to remain backward compatible. There are a few exceptions though:
- asyncio is a bit more stable than before, but it's still subject to changes.
- the readline integration in the apython script relies heavily on cpython implementation details (see [input_with_stderr_prompt](https://github.com/vxgmichel/aioconsole/blob/32fefda55fb64b42175dd310ccc7e7acc37631f8/aioconsole/apython.py#L140), issue #7 and PR #9)
- the asynchronous code execution is quite subtle, as you probably noticed, considering your last comment:
  >  I'm worried that whatever mechanism aioconsole uses to allow await at the top level might not work forever.

  My feeling about this is that the use of the `ast` module makes the asynchronous execution quite robust, except for this manual [indentation handling](https://github.com/vxgmichel/aioconsole/blob/32fefda55fb64b42175dd310ccc7e7acc37631f8/aioconsole/execute.py#L67-L69) which I'll try to replace some day.

  In any case, I'd expect asynchronous code execution to be added to the language at some point (since it is clearly not trivial). I did not make a feature request yet and I don't know if someone did it either, but it would probably be good to start a discussion with the core devs at some point.

Also, feel free to have a look at PR #21 to see if it fits your needs. Do you need a new release once this PR is merged?

For anyone else that is wondering, I looked into pdfminer and it doesn't appear to have a layout preservation option when the output text-based. I've used pdfminer to extract html to figure out the text placement of different text chunks, but from my previous work it isn't trivial to have pdfminer correctly order the text blocks from the html output (it places the `

` tags using absolute positioning and it is difficult to correctly infer the order of the text, which is a really important priority for textract).

I could see the code to sign certificates, but I could not follow a clear path from the webui code to the signer.

Based on your comment and the issue I ran into when setting it up for the first time, these are the two basic requirements I gathered:
1:  Allow the ability to distribute the signing and management functionality without having superfluous elements on the UI that do not add any functionality.
2 :  Give visual feedback when the user expects to see UI controls but due to some reason(misconfiguration, incomplete setup, expired CA, etc), they are not usable.

I believe the workflow most users that go from demo to production will start off with a single server for basic validation checks then move to setting up the secure infrastructure and segregation needed to move it into production(I’m just going to hope everyone does not have their signing keys sitting on a public interface).

I think easiest way to support the requirements and the workflow would be to modularize the functionality(OCSP, watchers, certificate revoke, cert renewal, cert creation, etc) and use the module setting( ie “Certificate Creation” ) as the test of whether or not to show the UI controls and enable the controller functionality.  A misconfigured site will still not work, but if a user goes to site.com/admin/certificates/add and sees that there is no root certificate or a warning besides it saying that it is expired then the reason for the failure and the needed remediation would be fairly intuitive.

 I am already changing the certificate page to make it easier/more intuitive to use in order to fit my usecase(multiuser env, userbase with low/no cert knowledge).  I am also going to be adding some other modules to django-ca to expand it from a pure x509 CA as well(openSSH signed keys do not use true x509 they also handle revocation differently).  I can put in some pull requests the UI to make the generic usecase more intuitive as well as adding some modularization.  Those two changes should probably be tracked in separate issues assuming you are ok with the general premise.

Thanks for letting me know. To be honest I was thinking about adding write functionality at the time of implementing the library but that would have introduced a few problems and the correct solution requires a lot of extra work. E.g.: retaining comments, perhaps formatting. This is why I decided create reader-only library which does the job for 90+% of project that don't want to manipulate the config programmatically.

Despite this I think write is a good idea. However in that case I'd structure the project differently. If write would be supported (correctly - e.g.: optionally retaining comments) then I'd implement the whole thing as a general purpose extended json library that consists of the current `JSONParser` plus a new `JSONWriter` object (that similarly to the Parser, operates with events - perhaps implements the parserlistener interface - and outputs json text in response to events).

On top of the previously described `JSONParser`, the general purpose extended json library should provide a `ParserListener` implementation that builds a python object hierarchy in response to events emitted by `JSONParser`, plus it should provide a utility function that can take a python object hierarchy and convert it to events for the `JSONWriter` in order to write json text.

How `json-cfg` comes to the picture? `json-cfg` would be simply another package that depends on the `JSONParser` and `JSONWriter` of the extended json library. `json-cfg` would provide another `ParserListener` implementation to build a config object tree (instead of primitive python obj tree) based on the events emitted by `JSONParser`. The loaded json object tree could also provide a save method which would be implemented by recursively walking the config obj tree and emitting events to a `JSONWriter`.

As you see this is quite a lot of work and I can't allocate free time for this in the near future. For this reason I'd keep json-cfg as it is. I thought people who want to modify their config and are fine reverting the `json.dump` can do that without the support of this library. E.g.:

```python
cfg = jsoncfg.load('my.cfg')
py_obj_tree = cfg()
py_obj_tree['whatever'] = ['woof', 'meow']
json.dumps(py_obj_tree)
```

Could you please get rid of the `TranslatedEmailTemplate` altogether? Sorry if my previous comment wasn't clear. It will be much easier if we just add `language` and `default_template` fields to `EmailTemplate`. So the model would look like this:
```python
class EmailTemplate(models.Model):
    """
    Model to hold template information from db
    """
    name = models.CharField(max_length=255, help_text=("e.g: 'welcome_email'"))
    description = models.TextField(blank=True, help_text='Description of this template.')
    subject = models.CharField(max_length=255, blank=True, validators=[validate_template_syntax])
    content = models.TextField(blank=True, validators=[validate_template_syntax])
    html_content = models.TextField(blank=True, validators=[validate_template_syntax])
    created = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)
    last_updated = models.DateTimeField(auto_now=True)
    language = models.CharField(blank=True, max_length=16)
    default_template = models.ForeignKey(self, related_name='translated_templates')
```

With this model, we don't event need to change much of the email sending related code/logic. We only need to patch `mail.send` to assign the right template based on the provided `language` argument.

Yeah, hash speeds can be weird sometimes :). My 2012 mac (2.6GHz Core-i7) runs `shasum -a 256` at 163MBps (so maybe 1.3Gbps). We hash the data as we send it (and as we receive it), so I'm pretty sure the hashing time will be completely hidden by the network latency, unless you're on a platform that has a really slow CPU but some sort of hardware TCP assist that lets it get a really fast transfer despite the CPU.

For the future, we could have a negotiation phase to the protocol (both sides advertise the hashes they support, one side picks their favorite), and yeah I'd probably lean towards Blake2 as an option over sha512, but I don't think the added protocol complexity is a win at this point.

I must say that Carel PCO are not always easy to work with. Too much variables... a lot just not defined....

To troubleshoot, you can use some tricks  : 
```
# This will read the object list... so you can see if there's something weird
bacnet.read('192.168.25.101 device 100 objectList')
# You can then make some tests like
bacnet.read('192.168.25.101 analogInput 1001 presentValue')

# Using bacnet.read, you are working directly on the stack... no device implied.
```

Also, for PCO, you should really define an object_list you want to use to define the device.
There are a lot of unused variables that will just pollute everything.

Refer to : http://bac0.readthedocs.io/en/latest/connect.html#object-list

Hi Johannes,
I just released version 0.6 of the module. In it I addressed your time stamp error (a) and your error from trying to get historical stock price data for ^FTSE (b).

a. For the time-stamp error, I re-factored the format_date method to properly handle unix datetime values older than 1/1/1970. I also added a check to ensure the method returned only a date, not a datetime value. Please let me know if this solves your problem.

b. The reason the module breaks when you try to get historical stock price data for ^FTSE is because that data is not available on Yahoo Finance. ^FTSE (and I am sure there are others) does not have a historical price data table. The only way to get this data is from the interactive chart. I have not had much time to look into this yet. In the interim, I added an exception handler around the _scrap_data method that will handle the error and return a null value for the ticker there is no data for to avoiding breaking your process. It will return as such:

{
  "^FTSE": null
}


Also to see this issue in action yourself, go ahead and navigate to both of these following URLs:

https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/^DJI/history?period1=741416400&period2=1530334800&interval=1d&filter=history&frequency=1d

and

https://finance.yahoo.com/quote/^FTSE/history?period1=741416400&period2=1530334800&interval=1d&filter=history&frequency=1d

The URL below is where that price data will need to come from for tickers like ^FTSE:
https://finance.yahoo.com/chart/^FTSE

FYI, I found a similar issue at https://github.com/UnitedIncome/serverless-python-requirements/issues/72 prior to their 3.0.0 beta version. (That project is derived from serverless-wsgi)

The approach they seem to take is:

- the "runtime" setting is used as the binary name: e.g. if your runtime is set to "python3.6" then it tries to execute the command "python3.6" locally too.
- this can be overridden with a `pythonBin` option

    ~~~
    custom:
      pythonRequirements:
        pythonBin: /usr/bin/python3.5
    ~~~

- the same setting is used for virtualenvs

    ~~~
    ./index.js:      const res = spawnSync('pipenv', ['--python', runtime, 'install']);
    ./index.js:      const res = spawnSync('pipenv', ['--python', runtime, '--venv']);
    ./index.js:      const res = spawnSync('pipenv', ['--python', runtime, '--venv']);
    ~~~

@dbrgn I myself wanted some `omit` and `fields` functionality (some fields are expensive to compute), and so that functionality is already part of our library.

Although @cb109 , what you wanted was omit and fields specified directly on the Serializer, at the time you instantiate it. I had a request for this here: https://github.com/dbrgn/drf-dynamic-fields/issues/13
I am sympathetic to that need.

I also wrote my own mixin that does this, standardising it somewhere would be nice.

However, since I wrote that feature request, I've found that doing this filtering on a nested ModelSerializer is very expensive. Specifically DRF's `get_fields` function on a ModelSerializer takes a long time to compute. Instead I just wrote another plain Serializer that is used in the nested context that only has the fields I want. There is some additional boilerplate with this solution, but all the logic isn't in the serializer anyway, so there isn't any duplication.

So, based on this performance finding, I'm not really excited about supporting nested serializer `fields` and `omit` in this library anymore.

Hi @endrewlai what you're doing is correct.

In your `process_m_message` function you will get messages for both 'bnbbtc@depth5' and 'ethbtc@depth5'. To determine the difference the this is a sample from the docs which I think you're using? https://python-binance.readthedocs.io/en/latest/websockets.html#id1

```
def process_m_message(msg):
    print("stream: {} data: {}".format(msg['stream'], msg['data']))
```

Where the `stream` value will tell you if it's come from 'bnbbtc@depth5' or 'ethbtc@depth5'.

The messages will come in independently though, it won't send one message with info for 'bnbbtc@depth5' and 'ethbtc@depth5'. You'll get one for 'bnbbtc@depth5' to process and then one for 'ethbtc@depth5', the other may or may not be in sync, just depends on what Binance feeds you.

Hope that clarifies things.

A finally solved the problem. To those who are interested:

Conflict between two library that support a common, namespace (javax.servlet) and subset of object:

    org.eclipse.jetty.orbit (pulled by spark-test-base)
    servlet-api (pulled by any spark related jar: spark-core, spark-mlib, spark-hive, spark-sql)

we can solve the problem by excluding the namespace  "javax.servlet"  from the compile/runtime phase in the build.sbt
build.sbt

As an additional point, there would still be a git replica in github but it would be under the OpenStack organization (https://github.com/openstack/python-memcached or similar). The changes would be:

* review.openstack.org would be used for submitting new patches/code review/CI testing

* bug tracking would need to be handled in launchpad until openstack's new bug tracker would be spun up and eventually bug tracking would happen in this (soon to be setup) bug tracker.

From a standpoint of what we're looking to do with this module if it is taken over:

* Continue maintenance on it, ensure we handle the outstanding bugs, etc

* improve module, as needed

* Maintain the current interface for interacting with memcached, as the module has a lot of drive time on it and uses in the wild.

* This would not make python-memcached "part of openstack", just a consumer of the CI infrastructure (e.g no CLA or other agreement needed to contribute, etc). There is a little more overhead in contributing due to the need to setup a user account in gerrit, but this is (imho) worth it for the benefit the CI infrastructure brings to us.

Reasons for why I have offered to take this over are simple. Pylibmc is very good, but is based in c-bindings, which means we have extra work in openstack due to the way we use eventlet and greenlet. The other options are bmemcached and pymemcached. Both of the alternatives are much more complex to use. I also know that some distributions have issues with convergence of "client" libraries in their main repositories, so keeping the options to a single case in "main" is important.

I am happy to answer/discuss any questions or concerns here before taking this over. Thanks for the open discussion!

However, this was a bug with this application, not a problem with pyjwt (they use ```algorithms``` instead of ```algorithm``` for the decode argument, but ```**kwargs``` happily ate the ```algorithm``` argument I was passing in, so it didn't raise a runtime error). 

Because this doesn't currently support public/private key encryption on the token, I can't think of any way this could be abused in the wild, as the attacker would still need access to your secret key in order to forge tokens. That said, I patched it and will release a new version with the patch ASAP, just to be sure.

Can confirm the issue: `update()` will generate a subquery, but `fallbacks()` won't work as a subquery because it relies on adding some extra filters. But when Django builds a subquery, it renames all tables so the extra filters break.

That could be fixed by ditching the `extra()` call and creating the proper internal ORM structure. That requires fiddling with the ORM though, which is not something I'd take lightly. I did manage to build a proof of concept though, so if it can be confined to a small and well documented case, it might be worth it.

In any case, when performing requests where you know you don't need translations, use the `GeonamesCity.objects.untranslated()` method. It will be (slightly) faster.

```python
    @staticmethod
    def imports(symbol_file, input_names, param_file=None, ctx=None):
        """Import model previously saved by `HybridBlock.export` or
        `Module.save_checkpoint` as a SymbolBlock for use in Gluon.
        Parameters
        ----------
        symbol_file : str
            Path to symbol file.
        input_names : list of str
            List of input variable names
        param_file : str, optional
            Path to parameter file.
        ctx : Context, default None
            The context to initialize SymbolBlock on.
        Returns
        -------
        SymbolBlock
            SymbolBlock loaded from symbol and parameter files.
        Examples
        --------
        >>> net1 = gluon.model_zoo.vision.resnet18_v1(
        ...     prefix='resnet', pretrained=True)
        >>> net1.hybridize()
        >>> x = mx.nd.random.normal(shape=(1, 3, 32, 32))
        >>> out1 = net1(x)
        >>> net1.export('net1', epoch=1)
        >>>
        >>> net2 = gluon.SymbolBlock.imports(
        ...     'net1-symbol.json', ['data'], 'net1-0001.params')
        >>> out2 = net2(x)
        """
        sym = symbol.load(symbol_file)
        if isinstance(input_names, str):
            input_names = [input_names]
        inputs = [symbol.var(i) for i in input_names]
        ret = SymbolBlock(sym, inputs)
        if param_file is not None:
            ret.collect_params().load(param_file, ctx=ctx)
        return ret

```

Hi @dingran 
I just noticed that you're using gluon interface with sparse ndarray. Unfortunately this is not supported yet. The gluon interface will try to run all operators with dense data/weight/gradient if you hybridize it. You probably will observe storage fallback messages in your console/terminal. 

If you don't hybridize the network, you can probably run it in imperative mode (I haven't verify it personally, so there's no guarantee that it works without any conversion between sparse and dense ndarrays..). Note that the `nn.Dense` layer uses `FullyConnected` operator, which doesn't support csr inputs (due to legacy operator interface that doesn't support sparse, we're fixing it). So it's actually converting everything into dense to perform the operation. Instead you can create a custom layer with sparse.dot operator which supports csr input to replace `nn.Dense` (note that the weight is transposed in FC though). 

Gluon sparse support is on the roadmap. Currently the easiest way to use the sparse feature is still through the symbol interface (unfortunately)

You cannot have a module named `zmq.py` because when you `import zmq` it will load your zmq instead of the installed pyzmq.

I found how we can fix it.  Before or after `socket.set`, we should call `socket._wait_write()`.  I'm trying to make a patch at https://github.com/what-studio/pyzmq/tree/pubsub-crash like this:

```python
def set(self, opt, val):
    """set socket option"""
    if opt in TIMEOS:
        warnings.warn("TIMEO socket options have no effect in zmq.green", UserWarning)
    sub = opt in (zmq.SUBSCRIBE, zmq.UNSUBSCRIBE)
    if sub:
        self._wait_write()
    result = super(_Socket, self).set(opt, val)
    if sub:
        self.__state_changed()
    return result
```

But there's a problem.  `socket._wait_write()` at the first subscription will never be woken up.  Because there's no another thread which writes something.  How can we make a socket wait for writing when only it has to?

@davehunt and @edmorley : Ed, you're right, this is the cause of getting 11.  The intent here was to get an overlap of one with the oldest resultset we have.  The reason was that we need to get the next 10 based on the ``push_timestamp``.  Here's the scenario we were trying to handle:

* If (and I'm not sure this is possible, tbh) there were 2 pushes with the exact same ``push_timestamp``.  
* In our current list of pushes, the last (oldest) one is just ONE of those two with the same timestamp
* we fetch the next 10 pushes with ``push_timestamp__lt`` (less than)

In this scenario, we'd miss the second of the pushes with identical timestamp.  The way we have it written here, you'd get an overlap of the oldest push, which we'd ignore, since we already have it.

This is one of those cases of, "It seemed like a good idea at the time" and, as I mentioned, I'm not sure two pushes could have identical ``push_timestamp``s.  That assumption may have been based on the concept that ``resultset``s could be groupings of commits from different repos vs. ``push``es only coming from a single repo.  Now that we only have pushes, that assumption could well be false and we could change the query to ``push_timestamp_lt`` and use the real count.

This might have a fairly simple solution. From graph.js, this is how the nodes are laid out vertically:

    /* Format nodes according to their depth and horizontal sort order.
       Algorithm: evenly distribute nodes along each depth level, offsetting each
       by the text line height to prevent overlapping text. The height of each
       depth level is therefore determined by the number of nodes at that depth. */

Looking at the task graph example, we see that this isn't really necessary. The first three nontrivial layers are spread out enough that we don't need to shift to avoid overlaps. In the next layer, we could get away with just two heights, alternating nodes like this:

```
node1   node3   node5
     node2   node4   node6
```

in the next layer down where the effect starts to get out of control, there's some secondary effect where each group is pushed down further. If we eliminate that effect and group them in tens or so, we could dramatically reduce the necessary vertical space. This will break down a bit with extremely wide levels, but we can break them into multiple rows or just stop including labels when this happens.

@danielgtaylor We need to do some more looking into this. Some thoughts:

* Adding a load to CurrentUser and then a has association to User based on the user name will produce an association that can return null/nil.  There is precedence for this, such as EC2 instance has Subnet.
* Calling GetUser with STS session credentials out-right fails. This forces a requirement on the user to know something about the origin of their credentials.
* While allowed by the resources.json schema, adding actions that rely on a potentially null/nil data member can also generate runtime errors. They can trigger client side validation for missing required prarameters and/or service-side validation for the same.

I'm not suggesting that we should not make these changes, I'm just exploring what the user experience may be.  I'm currently more inclined to add the has association to user, but not necessarily duplicating the other associations/actions. At a minimum, users can nil/null check the return from the User association method and stop there if they are using root account credentials.

As promised, here my findings:

WebDriver differences (when re-attaching to a session using that `webdriver.Remote(desired_capabilites={})` from the example code:
- Firefox Driver: creates a new session, but does not create/open a new window. Reusing old session possible.
- Chrome Driver: creates a new session, but creates/opens a new browser window. Reusing old session possible. 
- IE Driver: creates a new session, but creates/opens a new browser window. Reusing old session possible. 

The new window is opened, because the `webdriver.Remote()`'s `__init__` calls `start_session()` that sends a POST to http://localhost/hub/session with that `desired_capabilities={}`. Firefox seems to be the only browser that does not open a new window when this happens.

This is of course not good, i.e. we should avoid calling that `start_session()` because we don't want create a new session. Instead we'd need something like `ReusableWebDriver` that inherits from the `webdriver.Remote` but doesn't create a new session - and this is where it gets a bit ugly:
- to override `webdriver.Remote()`'s `__init__` you'd need to copy-paste it to your `ReusableWebDriver` `__init__` and comment out the `start_session()` step
- `webdriver.Remote()`'s `__init__` hasn't changed in the past years but if it does you'd need to do that copy-paste exercise again.
- In another words: more logical place to implement the re-use logic would be the selenium client.

However, I made a quick-and-dirty implementation of this:
- There's now a `ReusableWebDriver` that skips the session creation steps
- `Create Webdriver` KW  now stores the session info (session ID, command executor URL and capabilities) into a file
- When calling `Create Webdriver` KW  with `Remote` argument the `ReusableWebDriver` is created using the session info from the file.

The code is in my fork i.e. https://github.com/yahman72/Selenium2Library/tree/reusable_session

This seems to work with the browsers I have installed (FF, Chrome and IE), and I don't see any reason why it shouldn't work with all other browsers and the grid. There are, of course, some limitations e.g. with the runtime variables (as @ombre42 mentioned) that might not be "correct" on the re-execution. Thee kind of things aren't, IMHO, selenium2library's problem, but more a test-implementation planning topic that could be solved by organizing the Test development differently (like @aaltat wrote above)

**And now the big question:**
Is it worth implementing this for the selenium2library? That copy-paste exercise is pretty ugly...

If I would implement this properly I'd probably do it like @pekkaklarck already proposed i.e.:
- a new KW for storing the session data
- a new KW for re-connecting to a web-driver

This way there would not be any changes in the existing code.

If @vinaybond can see that click happens in the UI, but the action is not triggered in the application, then I would assume that this is a application problem or the test code problem and not a Selenium2Library issue. Because the selenium can be really fast and because the problem is more frequent on slow machines, I would assume that this some sort of timing issue. It feels that sometimes click is performed before the application is ready to receive the click, which is actually quite common problem. The assumption can be easily tested by temporally adding a sleep before the click. If the sleep resolved the problem, then it is a timing issue. 

To permanently solve the problem, without the sleep, you need to find the correct thing to poll. It can be other element, some hidden id or class or totally something else. Best way to find the solution is to ask from your development, which thing indicates that the page is fully loaded and is ready to receive the clicks.

But because this feels a timing problem and not a Selenium2Library issue, I am closing the issue. If someone feels that this is a Selenium2Library issue please send more details and we will re-open the issue.

Hi Dear Pekka,

One of the benefits of the Robot Framework Remote Server for me is that I
can register some of the common (shared) libraries among all of my test
projects on the server and tell all clients to get those from the server;
it eliminates repetition of shared libraries. I also have shared Robot
keywords which I prefer to serve them on a Robot Framework Remote Server
like what I'm doing for my Python libraries.

It may first seem that I can implement those Robot keywords in Python and
register them in the Robot Framework Remote Server, but the requirement is
reasonable when it comes to the keywords which use Selenium2Library
keywords. It's almost impossible to pass web-driver object between Robot
Framework keywords and Python classes/methods; if I want to have some
common keywords using Selenium2Library, I can't write them in Python in
order to serve them on a remote server. I have to implement (copy/paste)
them in each project.

Other than Selenium2Library, I am currently using a lot of Robot Framework
pre-defined keywords in my own written keywords and I don't want to
implement them in Python again, if I have to re-write them in Python to be
able to serve them in remote server.

I did some additional testing this morning and found a few things we might need to address. 

* usernames can be inferred from the access token and don't need to be in the constructor (we can follow the lead of the Uploads API on this: https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-sdk-py/pull/85)
* If the user's token doesn't support `datasets:*` scope, the 404 response and corresponding "Not found" message are entirely unhelpful in resolving the problem. We should handle this status code for the user and give them a more relevant error message as we do [here](https://github.com/mapbox/mapbox-sdk-py/blob/master/mapbox/services/uploads.py#L41)
* Features without *string* `id`s are rejected. If the feature `id` is not present (possible as it's not required by GeoJSON spec) or if `id` is an integer (another likely scenario), you get a HTTP 422 with a message that "Ids are required for GeoJSON features put in bulk". This seems like an odd quirk of the Datasets API that we should either fix in the API itself or validate and warn the user about.

Reading back through @dstufft's long post in more detail:

> - The syntax is something that's going to be familiar to all Python developers, so other than learning the "API" used in the file they don't need to learn anything special outside of that.

Fair enough. I agree that TOML and YAML are both less familiar, though many developers are used to using them in some other context. JSON is probably familiar to a lot of people, but its lack of comments make it a bad choice for human-written files.

> - It provides good support for optional, named parameters that don't feel janky (e.g. adding them to the YAML option requires switching to using an inline list or dictionary or something or you make the common case much harder).
> - It's more flexible which means things like with source() and with group() are more easily done and they can be more easily combined without having to add an explicit way of it.

These points seem to amount to a preference for Python syntax for declaring data. That's valid, and I agree that most declarative formats don't allow the mixture of positional and named values that function signatures do. But if this leads to having to scrape data out of a Python AST, I don't think the syntactic nicety is worth using a nonstandard format and special tooling.

> - This file is generally not a security sensitive one, it's not something you're going to get from PyPI, it's not something that you're likely to be fetching from random people and executing.
>   - At the time I originally spec'd this out projects like pyup.io, requires.io, etc didn't really exist so the only time this file was getting used was in an installation context.

This counters an argument against using Python, but it doesn't really provide a reason to pick Python.

> - Making it "Just Python" allows people to shim in things that we maybe forgot to add and then we can look at baking in more proper support down the road.

If we're considering parsing data out of the AST instead of executing it, this advantage would presumably go away.

>> Currently on PyPI there are 444,338 .tar.gz files uploaded and 58,774 .zip files uploaded.
>
> I don't think that necessarily speaks to the popularity of the format as much as the popularity of the operating system on which the package was produced.

Right, I'm not saying that people are explicitly picking ``.tar.gz`` (or explicitly picking ``.zip``) in the general case, but by the nature of there being way more ``.tar.gz`` than ``.zip``, we can make a guess about what the relative number of people who are going to observe *some* kind of difference in behavior. The fewer people who get any change in behavior, the less likely issues are to occur.

> zip files are easier to construct and extract in a Python process. Just the other week, I tried constructing a .tar.gz sdist in memory, but failed and punted, instead creating the sdist on my file system and dumping the file to a python literal string. I couldn't find an example of doing this anywhere. If .tar.gz is to be the preferred format, this use case needs to be solved.

Hmm, here's something I threw together really quickly for creating a tarfile completely in memory using only in memory files. If the files are already on the filesystem then this is even easier since you can just use ``tgz.add("/path/to/some/file.txt", arcname="example/file.txt")`` instead of needing to construct a ``TarInfo``.

```python
import tarfile
import io


tarobj = io.BytesIO()


with tarfile.open(fileobj=tarobj, mode="w:gz") as tgz:
    data = b"This is an example file."

    t = tarfile.TarInfo("example/file.txt")
    t.size = len(data)

    tgz.addfile(t, io.BytesIO(data))


with open("example.tar.gz", "wb") as fp:
    fp.write(tarobj.getvalue())
```

Similarly, you can extract all of the files of a ``.tar.gz`` using something like:


```python
import tarfile
import io

file_data = {}

tarobj = io.BytesIO(b"... Tar data goes here ...")

with tarfile.open(fileobj=tarobj, mode="r") as tgz:
    for filename in tgz.getnames():
        file_data[filename] = tgz.extractfile(filename).read()

```

>> changing Windows's default is more likely to propagate out faster.
>
> It seems like this motivation is the primary one. What if instead, PyPI were to display a warning banner for packages publishing the undesirable format? This could instigate the individual package maintainers to use later versions of setuptools or update their project config to match the recommendation (even if they use another packaging mechanism like distutils).

It's one of the primary motivators, but whereas Windows users are generally free to upgrade their Python or setuptools installations on their machines more or less at will, Linux/macOS developers have that shipped as part of their OS, making them unable to upgrade their Python or their setuptools easily. On macOS for example, to upgrade your setuptools currently requires disabling "rootless" (requires a reboot, makes your computer less secure), then forcibly upgrading it, then rebooting again to rootless. On Linux, the system Python (and setuptools) integrates with the entire OS, and by upgrading them you risk breaking the entire OS by upgrading that system.

It's also about the *number* of people who have to change here, the more people who experience a change, the greater the chance that change will break something (e.g. https://xkcd.com/1172/), I believe that changing to ``.tar.gz`` is the safer alternative.

Is ``.zip`` theoretically nicer? Maybe, it has some nice properties, it also has some negative properties. For sdist's usecase the two formats are largely equivalent with little reason to pick one over another (e.g., while zip may be better supported for decompression out of the box on Windows, a tar file is going to compress down to smaller, saving more bandwidth on PyPI and getting delivered fater to end users).

Oh, and TIL that anyone who has Python 3.4+ installed on their system, has a tool to create and extract ``.tar.gz`` installed (on the command line anyways), you can run ``python -m tarfile -e foobar.tar.gz`` to extract ``foobar.tar.gz`` to your current directory.

Historically, setuptools has been self-sufficient in the formats it knows how to install, this is to allow other systems (e.g. buildout) to easily bootstrap themselves by depending on setuptools only.

This means that, although you can easily add other build formats, like `bdist_egg`, by adding an entry point, setuptools currently doesn't have an entry point for installing different binary formats.

Still, if one is feeling adventurous, one could create such entry point configurability for installation formats on setuptools and take the opportunity to spin off the installation of `bdist_wininst` (and perhaps the building of `bdist_wininst` as well) into another package that we could put to pasture.

@ycartwhelen 

> why do you say this issue seems to be fixed

Because you reported an issue with Sobel filters, not SLIC. =)

Unfortunately, the requirement is there for both historical reasons and because it's useful to know the range of images based on data types for pipelining. See our [documentation on dtypes](http://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/user_guide/data_types.html).

Here's the rationale: when converting between image types, for example float to uint8, we need to know what the range of the images is, so that, for example, a low-brightness image remains low-brightness throughout its conversion, rather than get rescaled. So it makes sense to map [0, 1] in float to [0, 255] in uint8, and vice versa.

In your case, for SLIC, we need to ensure that the input image is of type float, which apparently we have equated with `float64`. So, when you pass in a `float64` image, the converter does nothing, while passing in a `float32` image triggers a conversion and subsequent error.

We could of course say, "hey, if it's a float32 to float64, don't mess with the range", but the way the conversion machinery is set up, we look at the input and output types separately, because otherwise you would have ntypes ^ 2 conditions to test, instead of 2 * ntypes conditions.

Either way, this problem comes up fairly often, see e.g. #1234.

I've opened a new issue that should fix this specific behaviour, `float32` causing an error in SLIC: #1737.

@stefanv:  To summarize the `dask` results, we can either go with either of the following:

 for a simple average:
```Python
futures = [dask.delayed(_run_one_shift)(s) for s in all_shifts]
mean = sum(futures) / len(futures)
mean = mean.compute(get=dask.threaded.get, num_workers=num_workers)
```

or for the running average:
```Python
mean = 0
for i, shift in enumerate(all_shifts):
    y = dask.delayed(_run_one_shift)(shift)
    mean = mean + (y - mean) / (i + 1)
mean = mean.compute(get=dask.threaded.get, num_workers=num_workers)
```

The running average is ~ 5% slower in my test case, but should have better numerical stability.  I don't have a strong preference either way:  @jni, @stefanv:  Which do you prefer?

Turns out stellar is not working properly with docker. All due to the fact that docker container exits when its main process finishes. But [here](https://github.com/fastmonkeys/stellar/blob/566bcc3ebf725b308568ea30b68a5758a886f98a/stellar/app.py#L181) we see that stellar forks main process. That's why it was working not properly. I have two choices:
1) fork this repo and remove that forking process part or maybe make this configurable.
2) use sh wrapper that checks that all stellar instances finished

I chose second option and my wrapper script looks like this:
```
#!/bin/sh

stellar "$@"

n=0
while true
do
  ps aux | grep -v grep | grep -q {stellar} || break
  n=$((n+1))
  if [ $n -gt 20 ]; then
    exit 1;
  fi
  sleep 1
done

```

I don't understand.  Here's the definition of a MappingSchema in colander:
```
class Schema(SchemaNode):
    schema_type = Mapping

MappingSchema = Schema
```

It's just a `SchemaNode` with `schema_type() is Mapping()`.  `schema_type()` is called on `__init__` if no `typ` is provided.

`MappingSchema` is just a shortcut class if you don't want to do `SchemaNode(Mapping())`.  They're equivalent.

Without this change, it's impossible to use a `SchemaNode` which is the basic building block of all colander schema.  Also, I don't see how this change would break any functionality as it just removes erroneously throwing an error on `SchemaNode` instances that would otherwise work fine.

(maybe the confusion lies with my changes to the tests...  the tests pass with or without my changes.  The changes are just to make things clearer and more consistent.)

Additionally, colander has the "feature" of allowing you to do the following:
```
>>> import colander
>>> x = colander.MappingSchema(colander.Sequence())
>>> x.typ
<colander.Sequence object at 0x7fb908fc6208>
>>> x.typ.unknown
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
AttributeError: 'Sequence' object has no attribute 'unknown'
>>> 
```

So, testing if a class is an instance of a `MappingSchema` doesn't mean that it actually has a `Mapping` type. (https://github.com/Pylons/colander/pull/168)  So, you're really testing the wrong thing with checking if a schema is an instance of a `MappingSchema`.

As a long-time OSS champion at CERN and a member of the task force which penned the original recommendation for CERN to adopt a default GPL policy, I too once believed that GPL and dual licencing was the answer. However experience over the past year of putting this into practice has exposed me to the complications and pitfalls, and I am no longer convinced it is the best way. Firstly, to dual licence you must have unanimous agreement of the copyright holders. The more holders, the less likely, easiest with a unique copyright holder; but copyright transfer was never cleanly established in v1, and isn't the norm in the world, quite the opposite. Secondly you don't dual licence to a permissive version, you dual licence to a completely closed one dedicated to the purpose, and what you define the "purpose" to be is complicated both legally and philosophically. Hence instead, to establish from the start a permissive licence for v3, without copyright transfer as it is unnecessary and undesired, that is just as encouraging of OSS contributions but in addition does not exclude or discourage commercial usage, is a more natural fit. It also aligns us with the majority of real-world packages that we ourselves rely on, and the OSS based business models that we see.

Hi, so it works in general like this on the first call to `ne.evaluate()`: 1.) if the function signature is not found in the cache dictionary, then it has to be parsed into an abstract syntax tree. This takes quite some time.  2.) Then the C-interpreter is called on the assembled bytecode, and if it has not been initiated previously it has to start up all its threads. This also takes time. So in order to get consistent benchmarks, a simple workaround is to run the test three times and take the minimum.  In this case the last two runs will always be faster. What I meant by 'junk call' is say, `ne.evaluate('b+b')` to initialize the C-interpreter. (Incidentally benchmarking NE2 and NE3 in the same Python interpreter is very tricky). 

NEON SIMD acceleration should work with NE3 out-of-the-box if auto-vectorization is enabled in your compiler.  I don't have one myself for testing. The difference between NE2 and NE3 here is that NE2 did pointer math, and put function calls inside loops, and unrolled the loops.  NE3 does array indexing and puts the loops inside the functions, so the compiler recognizes it and vectorizes it.  At some point hopefully Yeppp! (and Intel VML) will be added as backends, and Yeppp! is supposed to support NEON.

If you find problems with NE3 please feel free to raise an issue and I will add them to the test suite (which is the next task to do). Preface the issue with 'NE3:' please.  Strided array access for complex functions hasn't been implemented yet. I'm planning on raising issues on all the major roadmap items for NE3 soon but I'm travelling ATM.

**Try this command instead**
Without modifying your code, you should be able to run this command:

`python a.py B --arg1 arg1 --arg2 arg2 _imp create 3`


**What was wrong with your command?**
Here's what is happening now when you run `python a.py B --arg1 arg1 --arg2 arg2 create 3`:

`python a.py`: runs the a.py file, where `fire.Fire()` is called. Since you pass no arguments to `Fire`, it makes everything in `a.py` available at the command line. I think you're importing `B` in `a.py`, so B is available.

`B --arg1 arg1 --arg2 arg2`: An instance of `B` is constructed with arguments `arg1` and `arg2`.

`create`: Fire then tries to use the create argument to access a member of your instance of B, which fails since B doesn't have a method named create. If instead you access the `_imp` member of B, and then call `create`, it should do what you're looking for.

i was searching for something as minimal as this:

```
In [1]: from rdflib import *
INFO:rdflib:RDFLib Version: 4.2.1

In [2]: data = """{
  "@id": "urn:foo",
  "http://chemsem.com/number": 14.00307400478
}"""

In [3]: g = Graph().parse(data=data, format='json-ld')

In [4]: list(g)
Out[4]:
[(rdflib.term.URIRef(u'urn:foo'),
  rdflib.term.URIRef(u'http://chemsem.com/number'),
  rdflib.term.Literal(u'14.0030740048', datatype=rdflib.term.URIRef(u'http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#double')))]

In [5]: print g.serialize(format="turtle")
@prefix ns1: <http://chemsem.com/> .
@prefix rdf: <http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#> .
@prefix rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#> .
@prefix xml: <http://www.w3.org/XML/1998/namespace> .
@prefix xsd: <http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#> .

<urn:foo> ns1:number 1.400307e+01 .


In [6]: print g.serialize(format="nt")
<urn:foo> <http://chemsem.com/number> "14.0030740048"^^<http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#double> .

```

Some tests show that this behavior originates from [turtle.py/TurtleSerializer/label()](https://github.com/RDFLib/rdflib/blob/master/rdflib/plugins/serializers/turtle.py#L304):

```
In [13]: l = list(g)[0][2]

In [14]: l.n3()
Out[14]: u'"14.0030740048"^^<http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#double>'

In [15]: l._literal_n3()
Out[15]: u'"14.0030740048"^^<http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#double>'

In [16]: l._literal_n3(use_plain=True)
Out[16]: u'1.400307e+01'
```

To be honest, I'm not entirely sure whether this is a bug or a feature, but i can see that it is annoying in your case. IIRC this is designed to make `Literal(1)` end up as `1` in ttl output, so simply switching it by default will cause trouble for people relying on that:
```
In [17]: Literal(1).n3()
Out[17]: u'"1"^^<http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#integer>'

In [18]: Literal(1)._literal_n3(use_plain=True)
Out[18]: u'1'
```

On the other hand making this easier to configure wouldn't hurt that much...

Hi!

Thanks for being part of the Font Awesome Community.

The first error to fix is that you are loading two versions of font awesome (probably one comes from your theme):

```html
<link rel='stylesheet' id='hamlet-lite-font-awesome-css-css'  href='https://gallantlygal.com/wp-content/themes/hamlet-lite/css/font-awesome.css?ver=4.9.4' type='text/css' media='all' />
<!-- ... -->
<link href="https://maxcdn.bootstrapcdn.com/font-awesome/4.7.0/css/font-awesome.min.css" rel="stylesheet" integrity="sha384-wvfXpqpZZVQGK6TAh5PVlGOfQNHSoD2xbE+QkPxCAFlNEevoEH3Sl0sibVcOQVnN" crossorigin="anonymous">
```

If you want to use FA 4.7.0, you can remove the second link because your theme is loading the very same version

The most !important thing, is that you get rid of this rule: 

```css
i {
    font-family: 'Cormorant Garamond'!important;
    font-style: italic!important;
    font-weight: 400!important;
}
```

It is part of your html, at the end of the head tag.

As a workaround, you can use "span" as a tag for font awesome icons, but you should really consider to remove that rule.

Hope it helps

So, I'm specifically working on this.  It seems like there's two potential pathways forwards:

1. write to the shared information service (SIS) with the information about the manual failover
2. send signals to the API on each node about the failover

SIS Appoach:
Advantages:
* client only needs to access the SIS
* easy to integrate with SIS-based monitoring
* consistency with other signals we may want to pass via the SIS, i.e. "nogovernor" flag
* allows signalling other replicas not to try to grab the master flag
* requires only a very simple client script

Disadvantages:
* very asynchronous; hard to know when failover is complete except by polling
* zero troubleshooting info if failover doesn't happen
* requires admins writing to the SIS, which is a new pattern

Node API approach
Advantages:
* synchronous: can find out how failover is going immediately
* doesn't require touching the main failover loop
* makes targeting specific nodes fairly obvious

Disadvangates:
* requires securing the node API because now you can change databases via it.
* client would need to be heavy; will have to connect to all database nodes, possibly, to make the failover go as planned
* possible synchronization issues between data in SIS and what individual nodes are doing

Overall, it seems to me that doing this via the SIS makes more sense.  Discussion?

The risk I see with declarative formulas, totally out of the python code, is that it will work for some easy cases (like cotisations), but clearly we won't be able to do it for more complicated formulas. So we'll end up having two codebases: one in yaml, another in python. 

I'm sure we'll still be able to run products like mes-aides or embauche, confined to their own respective domain. 

But for developers, it will be hard to understand these two different world. They will probably work in only one of them, and not be able to do much on the other one. And thus we will be giving up the idea of a single product to make the French tax and benefit system readable and executable.

Looking at the output of the 3 on an already flaked code:
- The main impact of `black` and `yapf` seems to be to enforce a max line length and to handle that consistently. In some cases, the result definitely looks nicer. In others, it's arguable. 
- `autopep8` would just allow to automate the fix of flake8 errors we usually deal with manually. 

I must say I'm so far not fully sure to see the benefits of an advanced code formatter. I can totally be convinced, and adapt if other regular contributors see benefits in it, or if we think that formatters are so standard that not having one makes our codebase look dirty. But if I come back to #702, the two things we wanted to automate were:
- Making sure no `u'` are added to the code.
    - Only `black` can automatically fix that, so we'd have to adopt all its conventions
    - A `grep -e \\bu\' -e \\bu\" **/*.py` could detect them, and there may be a way to autofix in shell to. Agreed, it's much more artisanal than a dedicated formatter. 
- Automatically add future imports to the code
    - Neither `black` nor `yapf` can do that
    - In 4 months, we won't care as we'll stop supporting Python 2

Hi, the reason we have this `AlreadyEndedException ` exception is because sometimes users have race condition or bugs result in ending the segment before attaching a new subsegment. 

You can still close the segment while leave subsegments opening, but if a subsegment is created after segment close, it probably is not considered part of the logical operation.

In this case, if Flask already close the segment, it means the request is already served and a response is returned. The session flush happens after response seems should not be part of a request/response cycle so this operation probably should not be captured, instead of randomly being dropped due to `AlreadyEndedException `.

But my question is that should Flask even close the segment when it doesn't know flush succeed or not? What happen if a request mutates some data and gets a 200 but back-end database write fails? I'm little confused.

To make sure we get the right fix I would like to first understand the use case of how recording flush would be useful in this case.

There is no single definition of what consitutes private/public in Python.

In general, for more established and matured projects, documented API and documented usage patterns (e.g. this class is public API, but instantiation should only happen through this other class) constitutes the public API, and everything else is not. Naming conventions and things like `__all__` surely help here, but those are never leading and often insufficient anyway to express the intentions of the author/maintainer.
