If this isn't acceptable as a change in the repo, anyone wanting to do the same should be able to use this snippet in their code:

```python
import base64

from google.cloud.datastore import _app_engine_key_pb2
from google.cloud.datastore.key import _to_legacy_path


def to_proper_urlsafe(key, location_prefix=None):
    """Convert to a base64 encode urlsafe string for App Engine.

    This is intended to work with the "legacy" representation of a
    datastore "Key" used within Google App Engine (a so-called
    "Reference"). The returned string can be used as the ``urlsafe``
    argument to ``ndb.Key(urlsafe=...)``. The base64 encoded values
    will have padding removed.
    .. note::
            The string returned by ``to_legacy_urlsafe`` is equivalent, but
            not identical, to the string returned by ``ndb``. The location
            prefix may need to be specified to obtain idendentical urlsafe
            keys.

    :type key: google.cloud.datastore.key.Key
    :param key: The key that you wish to convert to urlsafe

    :type location_prefix: str
    :param location_prefix: The location prefix of ones project. Often
                            this value is 's~', but may be 'dev~', 'e~', or
                            other location prefixes currently unknown.
    :rtype: bytes
    :returns: A bytestring containing the key encoded as URL-safe base64.
    """
    if location_prefix:
        project_id = location_prefix + key._project
    else:
        project_id = key._project

    reference = _app_engine_key_pb2.Reference(
        app=project_id,
        path=_to_legacy_path(key._path),  # Avoid the copy.
        name_space=key.namespace,
    )
    raw_bytes = reference.SerializeToString()
    return base64.urlsafe_b64encode(raw_bytes)
```

```
query_builder.getLimitBuilder().setValue(500000);

The reasoning here is that [proto3](https://developers.google.com/protocol-buffers/) treats an unset primitive and a primitive set to the default value as being equivalent. For `limit`, we need to use the `Int32Value` type to distinguish between a query with no limit and a query with `limit=0`.

We fixed it!!!

The issue was that the decorator created the new class in its own module rather than the module of the original class.

By defining the ```__module__``` attribute of the decorated class to be that from the incoming undecorated class, both pickle and dill are now happy.

Hey @emadrid - I haven't had a chance to dive into this after my pull request earlier last year.  Honestly it'd take a little bit of work, since I don't believe Cloudformation itself will allow Ref's directly inside of an ARN (at least I can't figure out how it would), so you'd have to have awacs detect the `Ref`, and then produce a sort of `Join` with all the parts.

For now, that's probably the best suggestion though - build your own ARN's using `Join`:

```
                    Statement(
                        Action=[awacs.dynamodb.PutItem, ],
                        Effect=Allow,
                        Resource=[
                            Join("", ["arn:aws:dynamodb:", 
                                         Ref("Region"), ":",
                                         Ref("AccountID"),
                                         ":table/", Ref("TableName")
                                         ]
                            )
                        ],
```

It's not as pretty as an AWACS Arn, but it should do the trick.

It's worth noting that the SecurityGroup[Ingress|Egress] `resources` (https://github.com/cloudtools/troposphere/blob/master/troposphere/ec2.py#L236) are different than the SecurityGroupRule `property` (https://github.com/cloudtools/troposphere/blob/master/troposphere/ec2.py#L273).

The former is a resource, and used for adding rules to an existing SecurityGroup (either created in the template separately, or previously created) and the latter is for adding rules at the creation time of the security group (a use of that can be seen here: https://github.com/remind101/stacker/blob/master/stacker/blueprints/bastion.py#L56)

Yeah - it should be named `--config-overlay`.  FWIW - when you just want a pex of an official release: `pex -v -c pants pantsbuild.pants -o pants` is the way to go.  This assumes you have pex installed (`pip install pex`).

For example (I use pyenv to isolate python installed tools, but thats not really a relevant detail here):
```
~ $ mkdir /tmp/build && cd /tmp/build
/tmp/build $ pyenv activate pex

(pex)/tmp/build $ pex --version
pex 1.1.1

(pex)/tmp/build $ pex -v -c pants pantsbuild.pants -o pants
  cov-core 1.15.0 :: Resolving distributions :: Packaging cov-core                  
  pytest-cov 1.8.1
  setuptools 5.4.1
  Markdown 2.1.1

NB: You would either need to run the command once on a machine of each platform you distribute to, or else you would need to have a repository of pre-built eggs or wheels for platform-specific libs pants uses, like pycov and psutil and use pex's `--find-links` flag to point it at that repo and `--platform` to tell it which platform to pull platform-specific binary distributions for.

> Having a general error when using python3 to run the pex file generated by buildkite.

Problem fixed by creating pex file inside the build_whl.sh instead of using .buildkite/build_pex.sh to generate pex file. It is because .buildkite/build_pex.sh generates the pex file inside the actual host, where pex version is only 1.1.2, instead of inside a docker container. 
After this fix, pex file downloaded from buildkite runs successfully with Python3.5 and Python3.6 as well.

This problem will usually arrive in requests that are not originated from the main HTML file, but from a secondary source such as an image or font file being requested from withing a CSS file that is included in the HTML page.

A quick and dirty solution for now would be to store last known `referrerDatetime` in the `localStorage` and use that when `referrerDatetime` is null.

> With that said, is there any way for us to express in the ultimate representation of the root memento that a forward occurred? HTTP 3XX are often automatically followed by a browser whereas the redirect would be more evident with a non-browser-based User-Agent like curl.

This seemingly simple question is more involved than it feels it is. A composite memento is made up of dozens and some times hundreds of embedded resources. How many redirection actions do we want to report? Redirection to the closest mementos happen all the time, especially for the embedded resources that are often several folds more than root HTML pages. Unless entire page is captured at once and rewritten to point to corresponding datetime values of every embedded resource before storing (along the lines of how archive.is does it), there is no good way of knowing absolute final non-redirecting URI-Ms for every resource from the client side in advance. In a normal archival browsing, we request an HTML page with our desired datetime which then redirects us to the closest root memento which then requests every embedded resource with the same datetime as the served (and potentially redirected) root memento. These requests then silently get redirected to the nearest available captures of each embedded resource. This lazy datetime resolution is almost essential to function archival replay, especially when we strive for no-rewriting for the replay sake.

If we do want to express that a redirect happened, the best way would be to take note of the requested datetime on each navigation request and save it in the localstorage, then add a banner on the root memento to tell what was the requested datetime, how long before or after the served memento was captured and how much is the temporal spread of various embedded resources with respect to the requested datetime or the served root memento datetime.

This is because a layer under the top layer has an

```yaml
ignore:
  - 'README.md'
```

While the easy solution is to just remove that line, the ability to do an ignore per layer instead of globally is absolutely necessary. In addition, being able to ignore files from the top layer in lower layers seems equally as important. I'd like to propose the following.

First, that a new `layer-ignore` key is created, which will be a dict of layer names that are a list of files to ignore at that layer:

```yaml
layer-ignore:
  layer-name:
    - 'FILE'
    - 'path/file'
  other-layer:
    - 'path'
```

The second change is that the current ignore key:

```yaml
ignore:
  - 'README.md'
```

would be transformed to

```yaml
layer-ignore:
  layer-name:
    - 'README.md'
```

Thoughts?

PyAMG to carry out it's nodal coarsening, and do block relaxation.  If it

matrix, then you can always try a straight CSR matrix, and let PyAMG try
and figure out the rest.

ExaBGP does not implement threat as withdraw on errors - it would not be hard to do - it is just that I need to read the whole RFC as you should implement it all - not just that bit.

The RFC wording says: SHALL (which is SHOULD) and is not a mandatory requirement for compliance. So I am aware we do not handle this ideally but we are implementing the RFC in a valid way.

To give you some background on why treat-as-withdraw exists:

large community is a new attribute, it will be the newest one to be created when the draft goes to RFC. 
When ASN4 (which required a new attribute to encode the 32 bit ASN path) was created it was found that a speaker could generate the route, the receiver (its configured peer) could be fine with it, as for it, it was transitive and unknown - so it would not be decoded, transmit it further.
It would found that in the wild some routers would have issue decoding this attribute as it had been mal-formed at the source. Something the initial peer could not know.
So the FIRST ASN4 aware speaker, would receive this packet, see it was invalid, and tear down the session. 
The session had nothing to do with the announcer (ie: session were flapping a few ASN away from the announcer): Imagine a T1 which get that attribute from a client, propagate it to another T1 which notice the issue. You could just cause one/multiple 100Gb interface to drop just due to a small client mistake.

So, to prevent this scenario, it was decided that while the 'normal' tear down session on invalid data had worked well, it was in that case better to loose the route.

I agree that I should implement the RFC .. perhaps you can open another issue for that.

You can read more about the initial issue (which I simplied) here:
http://rob.sh/post/175

Yet another related thing is: we really should get automatic base64 encoding in binary payloads on JSON transports added in the course: https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/issues/574

Note that is _related_, but only needed when one of the WAMP clients using payload transparency (with binaries) is using JSON. When both clients speak the same binary capable serializer, the (binary) app payload item in the respective WAMP messages won't be touched.

The same will then apply for Crossbar.io with a MQTT client publishing a binary payload: WAMP clients using a binary capable serializer will get the MQTT payload untouched and unprocessed. For WAMP clients that speak JSON, the MQTT payload will be transofrmed according to #574 

However, AutobahnJS historically only supported JSON, but now it support MessagePack and CBOR too, so this is not overly pressing.

But the WAMP spec has it, so we should add it. See "15. Binary conversion of JSON Strings" https://github.com/wamp-proto/wamp-proto/blob/master/rfc/draft-oberstet-hybi-crossbar-wamp.txt#L7379

This was for reproducibility.  Before the days of beaker, it was really easy to lose track of what experiment used what parameters, and so logging all of them seemed like a good idea.  I still think we should log all of them, including default values, though I'm not opposed to switching it to just `info`.  The benefit of a separate logging level is that it's theoretically easy to grep the log file for parameter lines, and with some simple shell commands you could create a hocon file that would work to reproduce the experiment.  We could get the same thing by prepending something like `PARAM: ` to the info statement, or by looking at the class that logged the message.  The drawback of the separate logging level (in addition to what @schmmd mentioned) is that it required us to pin sphinx, because the newer version of sphinx uses python loggers in a way that treats our `PARAM` level as an error, and so `html-strict` fails to build.

Sorry to drop in so late: this is a result of the requirement in the multitrack midifile standard: tempo related information must reside in the first track. Most applications writing multitrack midi file will create a separate track (a conductor track) that _only_ holds tempo and related information.
Many applications reading multitrack midi files will actually expect timing/tempo information in such a sepatate track. Note also that SMF doesn't even have a data structure representing a _score_.

This looks to be a chicken-and-egg problem with the healthcheck code. When we push an app with HEALTHCHECK_STATUS_CODE=999, publisher will not publish the app because a GET / will return a 200 response, not a 999 response. The router then has no containers published, so it won’t template the app, causing it to return 404s. Dynamic systems have stable and unstable fixed points. Without noise these systems would be driven to one of their stable fixed points and stay there for ever. So, fixed points resemble an equilibrium state.

Noise pushes the system out of their equilibrium and the system's dynamics try to bring it back. So, by averaging the change of state we are able to model the deterministic dynamics as polynomial. Its roots are the fixed points of the dynamics. We are taking the largest fixed point. In the dissipative soliton example, this is an estimate for the equilibrium velocity of the dissipative soliton.

I give my 2cents to this issue, although I am not an expert in signal processing. Tbh, my following conclusions are drawn from ~1h of research on Fast Fourier Transformation (FFT). Please correct me if I am mistaken.

I suspect you might have a misconception about how FFT works. This is supported by the naming of your variable `max_lag`. This tastes like you inspect some kind of lag operator to be involved in the calculation, alike the computation of the autocorrelation. This is not the case. The FFT is only an efficient algorithm to calculate the Discrete Fourier Transform. I liked [this post](https://dsp.stackexchange.com/questions/509/what-effect-does-a-delay-in-the-time-domain-have-in-the-frequency-domain), it is stated there

> The discrete Fourier transform (DFT), commonly implemented by the fast Fourier transform (FFT), maps a finite-length sequence of discrete time-domain samples into an **equal-length sequence** of frequency-domain samples. The samples in the frequency domain are in general complex numbers; they represent coefficients that can be used in a weighted sum of complex exponential functions in the time domain to reconstruct the original time-domain signal.

The naming of the function `fft_coefficient` indicates that you are somehow interested in retrieving some of these coefficients and use them as features. As stated in the quote, for a sequence `x` it is true that `len(x) ==  len(fft(x))`. 
Your parameter `max_lag` corresponds to the parameter `n` in [`numpy.fft.rfft`](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy/reference/generated/numpy.fft.rfft.html#numpy.fft.rfft), where it is said 
> n : int, optional
Number of points along transformation axis in the input to use. If n is smaller than the length of the input, the input is cropped. If it is larger, the input is padded with zeros. If n is not given, the length of the input along the axis specified by axis is used.

So with your `max_lag`, you in fact input the sequence `x[:max_lag]` to the `fft` function. Of course, the results of `fft(x, 1)` and `fft(x, 2)` differ, because they operate on different sequences. Btw, in your example, 
```python
np.fft.rfft(x, 1).real  # val: -1.22464680e-16
x[0]  # val: -1.2246467991473532e-16
```
So the fft of only one value is the value itself.
What you might have expected by altering the parameter `max_lag` (i.e. the parameter `n`) is to get a "fit" of less FFT-coefficients than the length of the sequence, what is not how Discrete Fourier Transformation works. But this is only a guess. 

My suggestion:
- Do not pass a parameter to the `fft` method. This in fact truncates the input.
- Speed should not be an issue, because the complexity of the FFT algorithm is `O(n log(n))`, whereas DFT is normally `O(n**2)`. And, as a characteristic of the FFT for a real-valued series, [the second half of coefficients are simply complex-conjugates of the first half](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Discrete_Fourier_transform#The_real-input_DFT), which can be and is exploited
- Proposed selection procedure for the coefficients: 
    - Take the real parts or absolute values (magnitudes) of the first `max_lag` coefficients or
    - calculate the angle of each complex number and take the first `max_lag` angles or
    - sort the coefficients by real part / absolute value (magnitude) / angle and take the first `max_lag` ones
![Imaginary numbers in the coordinate system](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/7/7a/Complex_number_illustration_modarg.svg)
*r is the magnitude / absolute value of the imaginary number, \phi the associated angle. Use Pythagoras to calculate it.*

Regarding the async stuff, here are my current thoughts (which are already inspired by what you wrote):

Maybe we have different kind of base plugins: `BaseMainProcessPlugin` (runs in the main process such as `console` / `attach`), `BaseSharedProcessPlugin` (runs in the shared plugins process), `BaseOwnProcessPlugin` runs in their own process.

The `BaseSharedProcessPlugin` would need to provide an async API to play well in a shared process that is largely (or entirely) run in an event-looped fashion using `asyncio` APIs.

Both the `BaseSharedProcessPlugin` and the `BaseSharedProcessPlugin` would need to provide an API to create a proxy object to allow inter-process communication between plugins. 

It's still vague and needs more exploration though.

> here is what I think is necessary to overwrite activate.bat and deactivate.bat.

I finally got it to work: one must call `python setup.py develop`. The standard `python setup.py install` did not install activate.bat and deactivate.bat (not even when `--force` was added or `site-packages/conda` was explicitly deleted). Here are some additional remarks:

1. IMHO, the variable `CONDA_ACTIVE_ENV` should hold the full path to the active environment.  Alternatively, one could introduce an additional variable for that purpose. 

2. Why has the variable `CONDA_ENVS_PATH` disappeared?

3. I agree with @wulmer that there should be a (command line) way to put conda/activate on the PATH. The easiest is to create a new directory `<conda_root>\cmd` which holds trivial versions of conda.bat, activate.bat, and deactivate.bat that simply forward the call to the real scripts in `<conda_root>\Scripts`. Then a user can safely keep `<conda_root>\cmd` in the PATH at all times. As a bonus, this eliminates the need to copy the forwarding scripts to every environment. Moreover, a user could replace `<conda_root>\cmd` with another directory holding customized versions of these scripts (e.g. to change the prompt). This wouldn't work if activate.bat etc. were part of every environment (assuming that the environment's `Scripts` directory is at the front of the PATH).

Add packages both installs the (conda) packages and records the package name/version in the project file. environment.yml isn't used by default, nor conda env nor conda list. Those are all lower-layer "I want to do it manually" tools that don't arise when you are doing things on the project/platform layer.

I haven't heard anything about two ideas of packages though.

In the "project" layer, serializing environments isn't a user operation. Environments are always and automatically serialized. Users just add/remove packages and that affects both environment and serialized environment in one step behind the scenes.

There is an import feature to pull in an environment.yml to a project though.

> Why not using the original filename 

I use a hardcoded filename with a time stamp to avoid any conflict if multiple instance of OpenCobolIDE are running or when the user is working with split tabs and quicly change between tabs. But I could use ```%original_file_name_%timestamp.%original_file_extension``` if you prefer.

It's for the moments where something may not work as expected. There are at least two situations where it doesn't worked during my tests - we'll see if your changes and investigations around #168 fix this.

Additional ("normal") benefit:
If someone has a source folder on a network share and locks the view (this disables the file watcher too, doesn't it?) he can do a manual rescan.

...
```

This was originally ignored by filtering out everything with <family>6</family> since it was the only group with that flag. Well that is just all kinds of bad! I'm using the 1.0.6 version you just
submitted and I'm seeing all my nodes. Have you made any additional
changes? Nodes that contain <family>6</family> are ignored because they are
system nodes. That isn't showing up in all your nodes (in the XML from the
ISY) for some odd reason is it? As a tip, you can put in a a python
breakpoint by entering the following into a line:

import pdb; pdb.set_trace()

This will stop the code execution and enter an interactive shell. Type help
to see the options you have. I would recommend setting a breakpoint in
PyISY/Nodes/__init__.py line 157 and watching what happens in the loop the
second time around when PyISY should be importing the nodes.

PyISY variables do actually use VarEvents for their init, val, and lastEdit
properties. Is there something else you would want it used for?

Yeah, don't look into virtual environments yet, just know it is out there
for later. Python has evolved to have a MASSIVE ecosystem, which can be
overwhelming at first. One tool you will want to become familiar with,
however, is pip. Pip stands for Package Installer for Python. You can use
it to install any packages available at pypi.python.org (like PyISY). So to
install web.py, you can just type:

pip install web.py

However, since it sounds like you are using the default Python distribution
on the machine, you will need to run pip with admin privileges. To do this
in Linux, prepend the command sudo:

sudo pip install web.py

Hopefully that helps a bit.

@catalincatana what is the full command-line (minus any connection credentials) you're using with Curator?  What version of Elasticsearch are you using?
I've never tried this, but I do not think it would be possible without string replacement.  What's the use case that you're using environment variables?  Docker, or some other container-style?

I'm sorry you've had a bad experience.  You are, indeed, hitting something I never foresaw happening.

Is the box running Curator a very small machine?  Is that why 1.5G is a constraining factor?  Curator isn't obliged to run on the same machine as Elasticsearch itself, or even in the same subnet.  It only requires access to the REST endpoint of one of the nodes in the cluster.  This means you could have a larger machine run Curator without it requiring a larger machine.

I mention this, because while it is possible to alter the chunk size, I hesitate to do so.  Allow me to explain my reasoning.

First off, mappings that large per index are discouraged.  Indeed, if you have multiple `_type` values per index, that will pose a problem for future upgrading as the concept of `_type` has been removed for Elasticsearch 6.0.  In the future, Elasticsearch will only support one "type" (without using that terminology), or mapping per index.  This simplification will yield a tremendous benefit for indexing and searching performance, as well as index compression and storage density.  Consequently, giant mappings will be much more rare.  What I'm trying to gently suggest here is that your use of large mappings–and presumably multiple types–is not ideal and is likely to be a contributing factor, and that Elasticsearch developers have been working for a long time to gradually constrain such things as they lead to undesirable outcomes and performance issues.

The `chunk_index_list` function's primary function is to prevent the initial URL from being too large for Elasticsearch.  By default, the initial URL cannot be more than 4000 characters long.  Since Elasticsearch allows for providing a comma-separated list of indices to act on, the chunking function is there to prevent that default limit from being hit.  It apparently can also be used to limit the number of indices in a call, as you pointed out, but this was not its original intent.

Reducing the chunk size will increase the number of round-trips necessary to complete actions.  It is a tremendous benefit to be able to have Elasticsearch finish a batch of deletes in fewer passes.  A blanket change to the `chunk_index_list` function would affect the outbound requests, not just the one reading in the metadata–which, as I understand it, is where your problem lies.

A change to the `chunk_index_list` function to allow it to be selectable at call time is _technically_ a breaking API change, which are usually reserved for major releases.  While I feel like I could ethically change it in a minor point version, that isn't the difficult part.  The difficult part is deciding whether to make chunk size definable in a configuration file, or to arbitrarily set it to some low number at just this ingestion point, which helps you, but slows things down for other users and adds to the load of the cluster—which is a valid point with large mappings, as the cluster still has to isolate all of the metadata for only the requested indices.

As an afterthought, this issue is somewhat related to #795, and may be addressed in part by any changes made in the system to address this.

At any rate, I _am_ still considering this.  These are my reasons for being hesitant to change things.

You account that you are using for CLI is not the same as one you logon to portal.   The CLI is using service principal while Portal is interactive user.    Hence, you see the different user names.   We don't support publishing user for service principal to access SCM site.

You can, instead, get the bearer token for the service principal and access SCM site using that bearer token instead (Header, Authorization: Bearer <jwt>).

- it supports non-python use cases and shops so dealing with virtualenv is not always an option
- the script maintenance is a valid point but it already works and requires nothing to update for newer versions.  The script is a simple virtualenv bootstrap that uses secure pypi installation

The problem not in client session but in `ClientResponse` -- the response is closed in web handler before starting `StreamReadingPayload` machinery. But the response should be closed on exit from web handler -- otherwise lifetime of such objects becomes too complicated. Transferring ownership from web-handler to `StreamReaderPayload` looks too error-prone.

@takluyver Do you want to switch to CommonMark or not?
If yes, your argument doesn't make any difference if we switch now or later.
If no, that's a different story ...

I think switching to CommonMark is enough compelling advantage on its own, we don't need anything else to "sell" it. Also, most users won't even see a difference.

@minrk wrote:
> ... if CommonMark extensions do happen and we adopt them, then we would be breaking notebooks twice instead of once.

I don't get it.
If we switch now to CommonMark, we should try that the "extensions" we were using before don't change their behavior too much. This way, only "basic" CommonMark features will break.
If there are ever relevant "official" extensions (like inline math, block math, tables) and we change to them, there will be possible breakage there.
But a single spot will break at most once.

And of course there will be breakage if the CommonMark spec changes, which hopefully will happen extremely seldom.

But really, all this won't be such a big deal. Only some extreme cases will break when switching to CommonMark. And I'm quite sure that if there will ever be a LaTeX-math extension, it will look very similar to what we are using now (i.e. what is used in pandoc).
I'm not so sure about tables, but I also hope that there won't be too much breakage.

> adopting markdown-it + extensions *is not* adopting CommonMark, it is adopting yet another markdown dialect defined by a single implementation

TBH, I don't care what JavaScript library will be used, I don't have experience with any of them. I was just mentioning markdown-it because I found it by a quick web search and it seemed to be the only JavaScript library available that provides an API for syntax extensions (and many existing extensions using this API), all other libraries provide "only" an API for AST transformations and custom writers. But since the ugly hack for math blocks is already in place, that's probably not such a big problem. The only thing that may be problematic is the implementation of tables, which are not (and won't be) part of "core" CommonMark.

markdown-it claims to follow the CommonMark spec, so if they are not lying, it *would be* adopting CommonMark. The existence or non-existence of extensions doesn't change anything about that.

> doing so should mean that we are giving up on the prospect of actual CommonMark extensions in the vaguely near future

As I said above, potential "official" extensions for LaTeX math and tables will very likely be very close to what we're using now. They will probably be less restrictive, but this wouldn't be a backwards-compatibility problem.

> if we stick with CommonMark-derived implementations

I'm not quite sure what you mean by that.
Do you mean implementations that pass the test suite provided by https://github.com/jgm/CommonMark?
If yes, I agree, whatever library we choose should pass all those tests.

IMHO bindings should be (have to be) case-insentive. 

For example, there is no way to type capital letter `A` with a single unmodified keystroke. What you do is type `shift-a`. That is to say, the case of a letter is a product of its numeric key code and a modifier. 

This means that the key binding `shift-+` is invalid because there is no way to type the `+` key without a modifier. That binding (on a US keyboard) would be expressed as `shift-=`. 

The [phosphor-keymap](https://github.com/phosphorjs/phosphor-keymap) repo went live at the end of last week, and @blink1073 is working towards a PR which uses that to implement these shortcuts. It supports arbitrary length chords as well as scoping via CSS selectors (ala Atom). It treats bindings in a case-insensitive manner. It is uses `+` as the separator though, since `+` is not a valid key character like `-` is.

With that technical stuff out of the way, I definitely think they should be presented to the user in the UI in a readable form. My personal preference for this is cap-cased modifiers, and capitalized primary keys.

`Ctrl-Shift-A`, `Alt-3`, `D D`, `Ctrl-Alt-F5`, etc...

You described it right. The goal is that if before you had

```
export: foo
files: bar
```

you should now do this:

```
pick: foo/bar
export: foo
```

That is, `pick` requires a full (pre-export) filepath, but `export` will still trim the picked path to give you the same result you were getting with `files`. Can you tell me more about what you were doing with `move`? It sounds like you were thinking of a different model of how `pick` worked, so hopefully I can improve the docs to clear things up.

Note that the order of operations is always 1) `copy`/`move`, 2) `pick`, 3) `executable`, 4) `export`, 5) [deprecated] `files`. The order that you write the fields in your module doesn't matter, because YAML turns the whole thing into an unordered map, but I recommend this order for clarity. It might be a good idea for us to emphasize the order of operations in the docs, if that makes it clearer how the other fields relate to `export`.

To explain why we made the change -- actually I have to take the blame for this one, since @olson-sean-k preferred a different design -- the reason for the switch from export-relative paths (used in `files`, going away soon) to full paths (used in `pick`), is for consistency with other new fields. The `copy` and `move` fields have to take a full path and apply before the `export` step, otherwise you wouldn't be able to move outside files into the export directory. If other fields applied after the `export` step and took short paths (like `files`), you'd have a confusing mix of different path types in the same module. Making everything take full paths seems to be the simplest thing to do, though it means more typing when you write the file. Let me know if this seems like a bad design choice.

It would help me think about the requirements, if you could describe an example script that would consume this. Here are some of the questions that come up for me:

- Is it enough to dump everything out at once, or will callers want to do things like "list all module names" and "list all the overrides" separately?
- Would different modes like that work better as separate commands, or flags to a single command?
- Is the caller likely to be written in Bash (which struggles to parse even newlines), or a more powerful language?

If there's an 80% use case that can be satisfied by a simple command or two, that'll probably make sense to add into peru. But if we find ourselves imagining a bunch of different flags and filters and things like that (to do things like "list all the `url` fields for each git module that's been overridden" or "list all the modules that are defined but not imported"), it might make more sense to just tell clients "take a dependency on a YAML parsing library and read peru.yaml directly". That's one of the major benefits of using a widely supported format.

(Another thought that's come up before, is that in use cases that involve a lot of automated editing of the `peru.yaml` file, it might make more sense to actually generate the entire file, rather than trying to edit it in place. This is especially true if your automation is already storing state of its own somewhere else; it's much cleaner to have one source of truth than to have two of them and to try to keep them in sync.)

Let me know some more of your thoughts. I'll go ahead and comment inline on implementation details in this PR too.

This is a tricky one. As a user, I'm not quite sure how to resolve this ideally.

Running a real sync inside of the override still requires the user to pay close attention to how they're using the override directory, in much the same way as doing a `peru clean` in an override before syncing an external project. With the added downside of modifying the override directory in potentially unexpected ways, that's not not a slam dunk.

Another idea: Just ignore the `recursive` field for overrides. This _still_ requires the user to be intentional about the override's state, but at least preserves the WIP benefits of overrides. This way the user can decide whether to apply a clean sync for the override, or to make modifications to it instead — whereas forcing a sync of the override removes that option, and potentially interferes with whatever work the user was doing in an unclean override.

A downside is this could be annoying and/or confusing for users who are unaware of the behavior. So if you go this route, it may be worth outputting a notice, e.g. `Skipping recursive syncing for overrides`.
But still really nasty. We're going to need to work around it. I bet we could make a similar hang happen somewhere in plugin.py.

So you guys can do what you want with this, but I think this should be merged as I don't understand why #2451 was.

First of all, when did we stop requiring two reviews for `letsencrypt-auto` changes? They affect our users immediately so we need to be extra diligent so breakages don't occur.

Second, it appears that @erikrose was aware of this problem in #2490.
> The downside is that anyone still (deprecatedly) running le-auto out of the root of a (recently updated) master checkout will get a "Hey, the current release version le-auto I just self-upgraded to doesn't understand the --le-auto-phase2 flag" error from when we merge this until the next release is made, but that's better than a documented option not working right.

I hardly think this is deprecated. https://letsencrypt.org/howitworks/, https://letsencrypt.readthedocs.org/en/latest/intro.html, and https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt#installation all tell people to clone our repo and run `letsencrypt-auto`. The only place I found that didn't say this was https://letsencrypt.readthedocs.org/en/latest/using.html which provides no instructions on how to obtain `letsencrypt-auto`!

With this in mind, we have to choose between the following two scenarios until 0.4.1/0.5.0.

1. Have anyone who obtains a new copy of `letsencrypt-auto` following our official instructions experience #2490. This will only occur on their first run of the client, but nonetheless, their first experience with `letsencrypt` will be a fatal error with a message about a flag they didn't specify.
2. Let people who checkout `master`, use `--no-self-upgrade`. If they ever forget, `--no-self-upgrade` will be broken and they'll experience the problem in option 1.

In my opinion, the vast majority of people will not include `--no-self-upgrade` on every run of the client so this "fix" that causes confusing behavior/breakage for everyone is not worth it. If you agree, please merge this PR.

> Alternately, I've seen a discussion about having the Tor Browser recognize some kind of special-case certificates that are only for its consumption, not for other browsers, and having Let's Encrypt or some other entity issue Tor Browser-friendly DV certificates (that other browsers won't consume) for onion names.

A much better system is to have a single DV cert where one of the AltNames is the onion address. This results in actually providing one of the main features of TLS: authenticity verification. Thus, if you're BigCo, you can have your onion address share the same certificate -- thus verifying to all users that bigco.com and bigcoXXXX.onion are run by the *same people*. Tor already provides end-to-end encryption for onion addresses, authenticity is what's missing.

Using webroot installation will fail on all typical TYPO3 websites. TYPO3 uses a `.htaccess` in the webroot which forbids any access to files or directories starting with a "." in their name. This also applies to the `.well-known` folder used by letsencrypt. It is necessary to fix the Apache2 configuration and explicitly allow accessing of `.wellknown`, then webroot installation will work.

I just renewed my certs and this issue bit me again. I cannot set up auto-renew because opensmtpd is prissy: I *must* go in and manually `chmod g-o` the keyfile or else it refuses to run, since it is designed to run briefly as root and then drop privs. But that `chmod` breaks other daemons that assume they will be run as the user they are supposed to be. I don't know what to do. OpenSMTPD is doing the more secure thing---certainly we won't convince them to change---by forcing the more restrictive permissions u+r go-r, but daemons that don't do priv-dropping instead require g+r and group configuration.

I see that since I last checked in, `letsencrypt` now sets `privkey$N.pem` to 0640 (rw-r-----), supporting the latter case.  I like that, I think, but I also like opensmtpd.

I could use hard-links to effectively lie to smtpd about the permissions:
```
$ N=3  #which edition this cert is
$ cd /etc/letsencrypt/archive/comms.kousu.ca/
$ sudo ln privkey$N.pem privkey$N_.pem
$ sudo chmod 0600 privkey$N_.pem 
```

and arrange for smtpd to use the stable address of `/etc/letsencrypt/live/comms.kousu.ca/privkey_.pem` 
```
$ cd /etc/letsencrypt/archive/comms.kousu.ca/
$ sudo ln -s ../../archive/comms.kousu.ca/privkey$N_.pem privkey_.pem
```

Coverage decreased (-1.02%) to 94.123% when pulling **0f509ea23a241fcafd45816893607a9958ba2ef6 on fix/silence_warnings** into **606d054226148714e41be638d182ad4ac99f68f9 on master**.

It is problem between Tastypie and file storage of Django 1.10+.
After `PATCH` request, Tastypie re-save each attribute of current object and Django append `media/` prefix for FileField everytime, if path is not absolute. But Django started build relative path for media files also:

https://github.com/django/django/commit/fdf5cd3429369954e8deb764d9f30f6374581613#diff-87c0869f58253f571c08ccf0fc5c7465
```
Leading slashes in the second urljoin argument will return exactly that
argument, breaking FileSystemStorage.url behavior if called with a
parameter with leading slashes.
```
It's breaked working of method `PATCH` for objects with FileField.

I created hotfix, as override of `tastypie.fields.FileField`:
```
from urlparse import urljoin

from django.conf import settings
from tastypie.fields import FileField


class FileApiField(FileField):
    def convert(self, value):
        if value is None:
            return None
        return urljoin(settings.MEDIA_URL, str(value).lstrip(settings.MEDIA_URL))
```
It's enough for me, but creates problem with view of files in django admin.

So there are no sanity checks during `ToManyField.__init__()`, but further testing reveals that an `ApiFieldError` is already raised if you specify a bad attribute by string.     Parameters

    ----------

    ...

        The number of points with which to simulate an IV curve.

        If None or 0, an IV curve will not be generated.



    Returns option 1

    -------

    output: dict

        A dict with the following keys:



        * i_sc -  short circuit current in amperes.

        * v_oc -  open circuit voltage in volts.

        * i_mp -  current at maximum power point in amperes.

        * v_mp -  voltage at maximum power point in volts.

        * p_mp -  power at maximum power point in watts.

        * i_x -  current, in amperes, at ``v = 0.5*v_oc``.

        * i_xx -  current, in amperes, at ``V = 0.5*(v_oc+v_mp)``.

        * i - None or iv curve current. I'd also consider an empty array or missing key instead of None.

        * v - None or iv curve voltage. I'd also consider an empty array or missing key instead of None.





    Returns option 2

    -------

    output: dict

        A dict with the following keys and (voltage, current, power) values:



        * i_sc -  (0, i_sc, 0)

        * v_oc -  (v_oc, 0, 0)

        * p_mp -  (v_mp, i_mp, p_mp)

        * i_x -  (v_x, i_x, p_x) at ``v_x = 0.5*v_oc``

        * i_xx -  (v_xx, i_xx, p_xx) at ``v_xx = 0.5*(v_oc+v_mp)``

It is possible to use `Ref: AWS::Region` to find the region is the template. 
ex:
```
Fn::FindInMap: [LoadBalancerSubnets, {"Ref": "AWS::Region"}, Subnets]
```

However, it can be less practical  to use this in the user data.
It would be nice to be able to access the param passed to `--region` when calling Senza. 
Maybe with `{{SenzaInfo.Region}}` ?

Example for why it might be more difficult to access `Ref: AWS::Region` in the userdata:
```
  LaunchConfig:
    Type: "AWS::AutoScaling::LaunchConfiguration"
    Properties:
      InstanceType: m4.xlarge
      ImageId: ami-28fb711e
      IamInstanceProfile: configLoader
      InstanceMonitoring: true
      AssociatePublicIpAddress: false
      SecurityGroups:
        - Ref: MySecGroup
      UserData:
        Fn::Base64:
          | # '|' Joins multiple lines into one string but keep line breaks
            #!/usr/bin/env bash

            set -ve # verbose and exit if any errors

            echo "Ref: AWS::Region"  # <------ this will just echo "Ref: AWS::Region"
            # It would be nice to be able to access the param passed to --region when calling Senza.
            # Maybe like this?
            # echo "{{SenzaInfo.Region}}"
            # ... do more things
```

As a workaround, you can use this in the userdata:
```
REGION=$(ec2metadata --availability-zone | sed 's/.$//')```

```

Thanks! This works for me. In case you're interested here's some more detail about using App Engine and Cloud Storage:

There are two APIs for accessing cloud storage in AppEngine. One gives a file-like object, which works for reading tags. The file-like API only transfers blocks of the file that the application requests. Which is great because it takes much less than one second to read the ID3 tags in a 100MB MP3 file. 

Writing tags is a different story. Google Cloud Storage blobs are immutable (they can't be written). A blob update therefore is a read-modify-write. In the case of writing tags the whole MP3 would have to be pulled down modified locally and rewritten. Which can get expensive if you have long files (like two-hour DJ mixes). It's not clear to me whether it's even possible to do this in App Engine. If I ever try it I'll let you know.

Joining this conversation from #740. I'm in favor of adding a feature that allows a layer optionally  remember how its parameters were initialized. I think adding reset_params is a good use case for this feature. However, I think reset_params should fully work only if the network was defined with a  lasagne.init.Initializer. 

Considering the 3 cases for initial values: 
> 1) initialization function
> 2) explicit initial value (numpy array)
> 3) Theano shared variable or expression
> 

Let me present an argument for why this persistent initializers might be worth implementing only to handle the first case. 

Even if layers have the ability to remember there initialization method, I think it is reasonable to not require that they have to remember it. In this way layers can remember the simple implicit (function based) initialization values and can leave the more difficult explicit (numpy array / shared variable) to the user. 

**Why the first case is worth supporting:**
I think the first case is by far the most common. It is also fairly extensible because a user can subclass lasange.init.Initializer. The memory footprint is only as big as the Initializer itself, which for all standard subclasses is extremely small. Of course custom initializers can implement arbitrary code, which could make a very memory intensive Initializer, but the user is aware that at some point this initializer will be in memory and be used. I don't think much of a problem is added if this hypothetical Initializer persists, and if it is a problem, it should be possible for a layer to forget its initialization state.

**Why the second and third case should not be supported:**
The second and third case are equivalent to loading pretrained weights for specific layers.
When dealing with pretrained weights, the user must always explicitly load them and set their value.
It should be no different when using weights that have an explicitly set state that can not be expressed as a simple function. 

Of course, if the user desperately wanted their custom weights to be remembered they could subclass an Initializer object and the layer would remember it. However, this would incur the memory penalty stated above. I think this is ok, because the user is aware of creating a class with this large memory footprint and letting it persist in a layer.

**Pros/Cons of implementing a solution that only addresses the first case:**

* (Negative) nonstandard callable / explicit initialization wont work.

The main objection I see is that a reset_params method would not work on networks with parameter sharing. I think this can easily be handled by simply raising an exception if any layer is missing an initializer. An error message explaining that reset_params only remembers standard initialization methods could be raised, and I don't think that would cause too many headaches. Perhaps a flag could even be set to ignore those layers and reset params for only layers with "resetable" parameters ("resetable" might even be a tag that can be applied to params to denote if it knows about standard Initializer).  

* (Positive) The vast majority of networks will work with reset_params.

Most networks will use params initialized from an object that inherits Initializer because they are the default. Even when the a param is not defaulted, it is most often changed to another subclass of Initializer (like HeNormal or Orthogonal).

* (Positive) The network architecture can be fully written to disk.

In the case that the code that defines a network definition is lost, then there is no way to easily recreate the network. Having the initializer saved (if it is standard) allows for a portable architecture file to be written and live outside python code. 

* (Positive) Case 2 and 3 can be supported if the user wraps it with an Initializer object. 

Quoting from the Initializer documentation 
>     The :class:`Initializer` class represents a weight initializer used
>     to initialize weight parameters in a neural network layer. It should be
>     subclassed when implementing new types of weight initializers.

Having a quick and dirty way to support setting explicit values is nice, but in most cases is it preferable to implement a custom Initializer. The reset_params function should only support these standard classes. When subclassing Initializer it because the users job to ensure that it is memory efficient (maybe by keeping the weights on disk and only loading them when sample is called). 

* (Positive) Lazy initialization is a natural extension.

Another consequence of implementing this change is that layer parameters don't need to be initialized immediately if the layer knows how to do it. This removes the overhead of creating each parameter when you are simply defining your network. In many cases you I'm simply testing code that requires my layers to be defined, but I have no intention of ever executing the network. However, in these cases I still have to wait while SVD executes if I use orthogonal initialization. I believe all layer parameters currently default to some standard initialization function like GlorotUniform. Most layers in most networks will be initialized using these standard functions. In the cases that they are not the user can lazily load custom parameters into those layers as they would with pretrained weights.

I have a solution for this which will not cost anything if the folder hierarchy exists but if it fails then will create the hierarchy. It works for copy_file and copy_dir.

```
def copy_file(src_fs, src_path, dst_fs, dst_path, makedirs=True):
    """ extends copy_file to add directory hierarchy if does not exist
    
    key created on AWS does not automatically create parent directory hierarchy
    exists works but copy_file fails
    """ 
    try:
        fs.copy.copy_file(src_fs, src_path, dst_fs, dst_path)
    except fs.errors.ResourceNotFound as e:
        if not src_fs.exists(src_path):
            raise e
        log.warning(f"creating missing folder hierarchy for {src_path}")
        dirpath = src_path[:src_path.rfind(src_path.delimiter)]
        src_fs.makedirs(dirpath)
        fs.copy.copy_file(src_fs, src_path, dst_fs, dst_path)

def copy_dir(src_fs, src_path, dst_fs, dst_path, walker=None, on_copy=None, makedirs=True):
    """ extends copy_dir to add directory hierarchy if does not exist
    
    key created on AWS does not automatically create parent directory hierarchy
    isempty and listdir work but copy_dir fails
    """ 
    try:
        fs.copy.copy_dir(src_fs, src_path, dst_fs, dst_path, walker, on_copy)
    except fs.errors.ResourceNotFound as e:
        if src_fs.isempty(src_path):
            raise e
        log.warning(f"creating missing folder hierarchy for {src_path}")
        src_fs.makedirs(src_path)
        fs.copy.copy_dir(src_fs, src_path, dst_fs, dst_path, walker, on_copy)
```

I'm intrigued by this possibility and feel like there are two aspects to this proposal. 

On the one hand, having this information in an easily editable form would be amazingly useful.  

Laying out the information like this would be great for a variety of use cases (quick publication or keeping contact information available to students throughout a tutorial or talk).

But the display and the editing don't need to be handled in exactly the same way and their unification may not be good for all use cases (e.g., I generally don't like editing text at multiple different font sizes… I don't know if others share this dispreference but I am guessing that I'm not alone in this).

Fortunately, that seems like it would be easy enough to solve by having it visible in one mode and have it editable in another (perhaps triggered with a little ✏️  type icon that indicates "edit" in other cases). 

In terms of stickiness and ease of access vs. real estate concerns: is it possible to have an event trigger this to drop down attached to a small element (perhaps the same as the edit triggering element?), and make that element sticky (as opposed to making the title display itself sticky)?

Ok, let's bump this one. Here are my thoughts:

- Items should have ytid, URL or something so we can quickly load them without querying yt API
- In case link is broken (in example, the video is deleted) we should have enough information to search for alternative. A song matcher is already implemented in album search, so we can rely on that one. It uses song duration and title. There's an open ended question how to extract song title from song video.
- A nice feature would be to check playlist integrity (check if all items are available) and search for substitutions (manually or automatically)
- Manual matching if match confidence is low. Let user search for item and pick desired or let him skip that item if alternative is really not available.

I find writing JSON files by hand a bit difficult.  I prefer YAML when there is need for users to build such files manually. In that case, our playlist could be implemented in such way that it can work on playlist with only some information available. In example, only song title is required, and playlist loader should expand and add other fields upon loading the file, so a user can type in the rest or fill it interactively (either manually or automatically).

Also, I implemented a basic playlist reconstruction from text files in #361. I would still like to support that way and I see a few solutions.

1.  those two features implemented separately (I don't like this one)
1. add header, like #mpsyt-raw and #mpsyt-yaml, and trigger needed parser based on that
1. make parser guess which format is used (easier for users but a bit more complex code and I think that it's not worth it)
1. make some sort of convert script (built separate tool, I don't like this either)
1. insist to use only YAML style playlists

However I think I've got a temporary workaround using daemonized threads and signals:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python

import pcap
import signal
import time

from threading import Thread


class Sniffer(Thread):

    def __init__(self, sniffer):
	super(Sniffer, self).__init__()

	self.sniffer = sniffer

    def run(self):
	try:
	    for _, pkt in self.sniffer:
		print(":)")
	except KeyboardInterrupt:
	    print(":(")


if __name__ == '__main__':
    sniffer = pcap.pcap(name=None, promisc=True, immediate=True, timeout_ms=50)

    pcap_filter = 'inbound and dst port 65534'

    sniffer.setfilter(pcap_filter)

    t = Sniffer(sniffer)

    t.daemon = True

    t.start()

    signal.pause()
    # just to see the sad face if we got any packet
    time.sleep(1)

    print("aborted")
```

The main question is: is it enough to have it by bucket or per collection? Or should it be a complex combination of both?

Some insights on how it could work:

* Add a new built-in plugin (`kinto.plugins.quotas`)
* Introduce a new capability with exposed quotas values 
* Register a `AfterResourceChanged` subscriber that listens to every action on the record resource
* Use an internal storage dataset to track stats ([see example](https://github.com/Kinto/kinto-attachment/blob/0.6.0/kinto_attachment/utils.py#L146-L152))
* On create, sum the bytes, on delete subtracts, on update sum the difference between old/new. (*note: postgresql internal JSONB is going to be smaller but what counts is API input/output*)
* On create/update, check if the body size exceeds setting `size_per_item`
* On every new response provide the current stats in headers (?)
* On create/update, when one condition fails, raise the appropriate http exception

Then in a second iteration we can imagine defining the settings in the parent bucket etc.

Thank you. 
We are constrained by limited resources, but it is clear that we have to address this issue very soon as the Kernel becomes more "mainstream". 
To the best of my understanding, the new kernel exposes UVC devices with per-frame metadata as two video devices - one for frames and one for metadata. librealsense currently doesn't properly handle the "metadata" video device, hence the crash. As a first measure we will add check to ignore it as a work-around. Next, we will have to modify the library to take advantage of this new kernel feature.

These updates take into account the nuance of how the glob-ing based method makes listing files much faster for certain file systems (that implement analogs of `GetMatchingPaths`), but might have a different effect for other file systems. Based on internal documentation for Glob patterns, the glob-ing based method could raise errors for certain file systems within Google because the method could use patterns with multiple `*`s.

These new changes move much logic into `io_wrapper` so that they can be generalized, tested, and placed in a file that organizationally makes sense.

@jlazarow: You're asking to be able to attach a name that differs across steps or across samples within a batch? While that's a reasonable thing to request, in this issue I'm trying more to explore better ways to display the image data that we already store, rather than changing the data format.

That said, it would be reasonably easy to create a plugin that does exactly what you want! If you duplicate verbatim the code for the image plugin, but change the summary `op` function so that the emitted tensor is of the form (say) `[width, height, png0, label0, png1, label1, …]` instead of just `[width, height, png0, png1, …]`, then you could very easily read out those strings on the backend, forward them to the frontend, and display them below the "step" slider. It's unlikely that this functionality could be merged into the main images plugin, unless you can devise a data format that's backward-compatible with the current summaries. (I guess you could do something hacky like the following: observe that every PNG starts with `\x89PNG`, and instead write labels as `b'\x89LBL%s' % actual_label`, so that the backend can unambiguously determine whether each string is a label or an image. This would work, but I'm still not sure that it's something that we'd want to support—feel free to open a separate issue.)

If you don't want to write a plugin, here is an approach that is not optimal for viewing, but does "get the data out there": use a separate text summary where you provide the image names. This can freely vary across steps and across samples (the latter because the text summary can accept vectors of strings in addition to single strings). You would have to manually cross-reference the text summaries with the image summaries.

(A very hacky "solution" is to use a `tf.py_func` to superimpose the text of your label onto the actual image using `PIL` or `matplotlib` or something. Last resort only.)

Hi @lixinsu - do you mean you want to plot multiple lines separately in different plots in the same window? (something like matlab's subplots?) Or plot multiple traces on the same plot in a window? 

We don't support the former currently, but to get the desired behavior for that I'd suggest using multiple windows with a tag in the titles, this way you can filter by that tag and see them all. 

To plot multiple curves on the same axes you can determine the window name you want to use, and then use `viz.line(... win=<window_name>, update='append', name=<trace_name>)` for each trace.

Alternately, after https://github.com/facebookresearch/visdom/pull/283 is in you can plot all the relevant data for each experiment in its own env and then use the compare features to show the data on the same plot without needing to plot from different places into the same window.

The long answer is that you need to *either* specify multiple base classes (you don't need all: as long as you have at least 2 it's enough), or need to add the `py::multiple_inheritance()` tag.  See point 1 in the [multiple inheritance](http://pybind11.readthedocs.io/en/master/advanced/classes.html#multiple-inheritance) docs.

The long answer is that when you *don't* have multiple inheritance, a `Derived*` can be `reinterpret_cast` to a `Base*` (because the base class data is always at the beginning of a derived class; any fields the derived class adds come after the base class), and we rely on this in pybind.

That isn't valid with multiple inheritance: we need to go through a more complicated casting procedure to get to the base class.

But there's no way for us to tell (in straight C++) whether a class actually uses multiple inheritance, so we rely on the binding code: if you give us multiple bases, obviously there is multiple inheritance; but if you have multiple inheritance that isn't exposed to Python, as in this case, you need to add the tag to tell pybind that your class needs to take the more complicated casting path.

The same issue can be reproduced on Linux by changing Python's `dlopenflags` to `RTLD_GLOBAL`, which makes perfect sense. For pybind11's test suite, this can be done in `conftest.py`:
```python
if sys.platform.startswith("linux"):
    sys.setdlopenflags(os.RTLD_NOW | os.RTLD_GLOBAL)
```
This reproduces the issue when compiled without `-fvisibility=hidden` (i.e. in debug mode when building with CMake).

On the macOS side, this can't be configured at run time, but there's a [compile-time option](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/c82b7f332aff606af6c9c163da75f1e86514125e/Python/dynload_next.c#L14-L21) which looks to be enabled by default (at least with the homebrew build; haven't looked into others yet).

So it seems like there are two possible solutions here:

1. Always compile pybind11 modules with `-fvisibility=hidden`. This is pretty simple in CMake, but it would need to be documented for other build systems. The downside here is that the functionality of `py::module_local` is tied to a specific (off-by-default) compiler flag.

2. Add the visibility attribute to pybind11's namespaces. The nice part is that it's independent of the build system, but it has the downside of the attribute warning mentioned above (which may be annoying to track down and work around on the user side).

Right now I'm leaning toward solution 1. It seems a bit cleaner and it would only require user intervention for custom build systems in conjunction with `py::module_local`. On the other hand, the warnings produced by the namespace attribute would be a lot more common.

I think the problem comes down to differing meanings for "skip". RP assumes a test that is skipped is because something happened that needs investigation (precondition not met). In Python "skip" means that the test is not relevant for the current configuration.

With this code we distinguish these cases by whether the skip is in the test setup, or in the execution. Which seems to me to be the correct approach.

An alternative is what the java client does which is make a global option to keep them as "skipped" but mark them as not being an issue (https://github.com/reportportal/client-java/blob/2efdec2524b77a12c004cbf32780d8d8bd5a0779/src/main/java/com/epam/reportportal/service/LaunchImpl.java#L248-L252). I'm not a fan because this means that *all* skipped test will no longer be issues, which seems too far the other way. I'd prefer to give developers the flexibility.

@jayofdoom, thanks for the PR and sorry it sat around so long.

While we were initial excited by the possibility of simply using an IPv6 socket when possible to solve #1466, I don't think that approach will work. When reviewing this PR, I came across the following stack exchange [post](http://security.stackexchange.com/questions/92081/is-receiving-ipv4-connections-on-af-inet6-sockets-insecure). The tl;dr version is FreeBSD/OpenBSD do not map IPv4 traffic to IPv6 sockets and I have verified this is at least the case on modern FreeBSD. Since we support both of these systems with Certbot, this approach won't work.

Ideally, we can handle both IPv4 and IPv6 traffic transparently for the user. The best way I am aware of to try and do this (which is recommended in the stack exchange post) is to use two sockets, one for IPv4 and one for IPv6. The IPv6 socket has to have `IPV6_V6ONLY` set to prevent errors when binding the second socket on systems that do map IPv4 traffic to IPv6 sockets.

If you're still interested in and available to work on this, we'd love to see more from you here. If you're not, let us know and we can have someone else take a crack at it.

You're correct that it doesn't work as is. If your workflow is to generate a cert once using `certbot certonly/run --reuse-key` and then use `certbot renew`, `--reuse-key` doesn't have to be respected for `certonly/run`. It just needs to be saved for renewal (which to make happen is a one line change to `renewal.py`). With that said, `--reuse-key` probably should be respected when updating lineages with `certonly/run` as well.

I think jsha is right here. If we already had `--reuse-key` implemented in the way we'd want to support it, I'd be more inclined to take the PR since some people want that feature, but since this PR needs a little more work, I think we should just remove `--reuse-key` for now and just use `--key-path`. As jsha said, `--key-path` is strictly more powerful than `--reuse-key`. Anything you could do with `--reuse-key` you can do with `--key-path`.

```
certbot --apache
```
Your command:
```
certbot -apache
```
is interpreted as:
```
certbot -a pache
```
And ```-a``` is abbreviation of ```--authenticator```. certbot can't find an authenticator plugin called "pache", so it failed.

Hi @978740431, this is probably an instance of a known bug (although I'm not positive whether it's been reported here yet) where Certbot can't display the error message that the certificate authority returned because it quotes non-ASCII text from your own web site. This is normally encountered when your web site sends a 404 not found error in response to a renewal of a certificate originally obtained with `--webroot`, and the 404 page contains non-ASCII text which the CA quotes in the error message.

This normally means that your webroot directory was incorrectly specified or has changed somehow. (Of course, we need to fix Certbot to show a more useful error message in this case instead of the Unicode error.)

Well, it seems like this isn't going to happen. 

I did some digging in on how to reliably use the correct dbm module (of which there are four variants of) for various environments, I figured out that we'd have to roll our own. Looking more deeply into Apache `mod_socache_dbm` source code made it clear that there's no way to reliably predict the backend dbm engine flavor (that is decided in compile time), and as such I feel that we can't ship this feature. This would probably work in two thirds of the environment and installations as it is, but it's not nearly enough to justify a fragile implementation with user promises that we're not reliably able to meet.

It has taken quite a bit of effort, and I feel sad that this is the resolution, but it really doesn't seem like a great fit for Certbot. Because of this, I'm closing the PR.

I found the answer after some digging, this indeed is a difference between Python2 and Python3, a nuisant one at that.

It turns out the type Resource in the code is actually a named tuple. In Python2, `.__dict__` is added as convenient property wrapper of `._asdict()`, but this isn't done in Python3:

https://docs.python.org/2/library/collections.html#collections.somenamedtuple._asdict (find `__dict__` here) https://docs.python.org/3/library/collections.html#collections.somenamedtuple._asdict

So it looks like `._asdict()` is in fact the source of truth, and should be used for portable 2to3 code.

My biggest beef with the current setup is that a new user reading through the codebase has no way of determining that `LockedTransfer` is just an implementation detail. It's not even written in the [class's docstring](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/messages.py#L385):

```python
class LockedTransfer(SignedMessage):
    """ A transfer which signs that the partner can claim `locked_amount` if
    she knows the secret to `hashlock`.

    The asset amount is implicitly represented in the `locksroot` and won't be
    reflected in the `transferred_amount` until the secret is revealed.

    This signs Carol, that she can claim locked_amount from Bob if she knows the secret to hashlock

    If the secret to hashlock becomes public, but Bob fails to sign Carol a netted balance,
    with an updated rootlock which reflects the deletion of the lock, then
    Carol can request settlement on chain by providing:
        any signed [nonce, asset, balance, recipient, locksroot, ...]
        along a merkle proof from locksroot to the not yet netted formerly locked amount
    """
```

It is even instantiated in [some parts](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/tests/test_channel.py#L59) of the codebase which reinforces the idea that such a type of message exists. 

Granted, later down the code it is transformed into a `MediatedTransfer` but that's easy to miss.

If it's a code abstraction it should be treated as such.

I took some time yesterday to track down the issue, and here is what I got:

1. Apparently, `prefetch_related` on the queryset calls `prefetch_related_objects` (https://github.com/django/django/blob/1.10.1/django/db/models/query.py#L1387)
2. This function calls `prefetch_one_level` (https://github.com/django/django/blob/1.10.1/django/db/models/query.py#L1473)
3. At some point, this function retrieve a queryset from the prefetcher `get_prefetch_queryset` instance (https://github.com/django/django/blob/1.10.1/django/db/models/query.py#L1580). If I'm right, this queryset of related objects [is then looped](https://github.com/django/django/blob/1.10.1/django/db/models/query.py#L1602) over and results are binded to original requested instances
4. The mess seems to happen exactly [here](https://github.com/django/django/blob/1.10.1/django/db/models/fields/related_descriptors.py#L853): to retrieve the related objects, an `extra` query is used by the `get_prefetch_queryset` method, and it is in this method I found the wrong PK's of the related objects to be returned.

I'll take more time to clearly identify if the line linked in 4 is where the error happens, because this ORM code is full of lambdas, descriptors and other deep-vodoo-black-magic stuff that are not easy to grasp. However, this `extra` query seems like a good culprit to me.

For the report comment, it is easy to include it to reports, and it can be useful to let users know what exactly has been run. There are two caveats though:

1. Transcript is not available for remote tasks, and those are arguably the most interesting ones. I do feel a need to include trascripts in task files, because these reflects host-specific scripts and are very useful for debugging.

2. Transcript can be very long if we apply a script to thousands of files. A walkaround is to log only `__index=0`.

Transcripts are far less weight than DAG, and this is why DAG is only included with option `-d`.

I wanted the log set to info in this case to catch/record these events,
since these messages are not programming/communication related, but are
more to do with the operation of the magnet and hardware.

In some cases like moving in and out of persistent mode, it may take some
time, and the user may be wondering what is happening if there is no
feedback. Usually magnet PS controller should handle this particular type
of basic operation e.g. Switch heater timing for this, but this old model
doesn't, so I've added the extra function into the driver for safety/(to
ensure correct operation) as it's something that is commonly used in our
experiments (and would like to call in an automated routine).

Especially when working with equipment like larger superconducting magnets,
at full field there is a lot of energy stored/generated, so i prefer the
option to get some feedback on the magnet state, at critical points during
operation.

This log.info 'lower setting' is also optional (can be switched off,
reverted back to normal), for other users who do not require it.

Ah, 5 generations isn't very much time for TPOT to really refine the pipelines - at that point the GA has only gone through 5 rounds of selection. That's good to hear that pipelines from later in the run didn't retain this artifact.

The reason why TPOT doesn't immediately get rid of pipelines like this is because this artifact is potentially useful for building more complex pipelines later in the optimization process. Either of those `FunctionTransformer`s can be replaced with another pipeline operation in subsequent generations, and that could potentially be useful for improving prediction performance. As such, our pipeline regularization process doesn't penalize pipelines that make two copies of the features like this because it technically doesn't "hurt" the pipeline.

We've discussed other approaches to pipeline regularization (#207) that would probably weed out pipelines like you showed above, bu we haven't gotten to implementing those ideas yet.

First thing, that's an issue with `tnt`, not `vision` and can easily be solved by replacing it with `os.path.join`. It's as simple as that, and adding `path.py` as dependency won't make anyone stop using `/` and string operations. If they don't care to use `os.path` they won't care to use `path.py` either. It doesn't hurt to use `os.path`, and I don't find it less readable than an OOP interface.

I really don't like adding dependencies that are not needed. It's fine to do it for personal projects, but not at all for libraries that are installed on so many computers. Dependencies are additional problems you have to worry about. If they break, your code will be broken too. If they get deleted (see famous `leftPad` issue from npm) you have a problem and you have to rewrite your code and remove them asap. I'd rather stick to a simple and safe way with `os`.

> Does it exist already ? Something like make only_build node + make only_build go` ?

Sorta, but not really. You can prefix targets with `sdk/nodejs` to only run them for Node, but this is more of a funny detail of the build system and definitely not a planned feature.

> If I ship without building python, pulumi will not work with python programs ?

The parts of Python language support that we absolutely need to build for `brew` are written in Go, so we don't need `pipenv` for them. We do need `pipenv` to build and test the Python-language packages that Pulumi programs ultimately use when written in Python, but those are delivered via `pip` and not by `brew`. So we should be OK if we don't build or test those things when compiling on an end-user's machine.

In my mind what we need (and don't have) is something like a `make dist` target, where we compile only the things that need to be on the end user's machine and aren't acquired through package managers. This means we basically don't have to "build" the NodeJS or Python SDKs at all (other than the Go language host shims that I mentioned), since those are delivered via `npm` and `pip` respectively.

If you're using the Twitch GUI, you're using the `http` passthrough option by default (because some players on Windows don't support piping 😴). The `hls` option completely bypasses streamlink, and works not that well in combination with the GUI, so you need to change it in the settings menu first, which requires enabled advanced settings (Streamlink doesn't terminate the player process when its own process gets terminated, which is a feature and useful when using the CLI, but not useful in the GUI, because it doesn't spawn the player process itself).

Our thought is that the public api of the core widget classes will be the widget schema they implement, *not* the actual exported classes. Thus a change in a class interface will *not* be a breaking change, but a change in the schema they implement *will* be a breaking change. The thought is that there will be many more people implementing kernel-side implementations of the widgets (i.e., needing schema version compatibility), and not very many people extending the actual classes themselves (needing class interface compatibility), and that it would be too confusing to have two separate version numbers to keep track of (the schema compatibility and the javascript class interface compatibility).
