> Many of our internals methods are no[w] async, so even running synchronous code requires a loop/runner.

Ah, I get it.

> You'll have noticed that a limitation to use trio in nested-embed IPython will be that you can only run trio async code in it, but it's a limitation I'm happy to have for now.

I think it would actually be less confusing if the nested-embed IPython didn't support running user async code at all. If `embed` lets users run async code, then they'll think that it's `async_embed`, when it really isn't.

Maybe IPython should have a "synchronous only" mode, where user `await` doesn't work (basically like how it works now without your changes, at least from the user point of view). And maybe this mode should be automatically enabled by `IPython.embed()`?

A nice thing about a "synchronous only" mode is that it doesn't matter what coroutine runner you use, because the user can't see the coroutine runner anyway. You might even be able to use The World's Worst Coroutine Runner:

```python
def run(async_fn, *args):
    coro = async_fn(*args)
    try:
        coro.send(None)
    except StopIteration as exc:
        return exc.value
    else:
        raise RuntimeError(f"{async_fn.__name__!r} needs a real async loop")

async def cake():
    print("The async is a lie")

run(cake)
```

This works fine as long as `async_fn` never *actually* tries to suspend itself – it can `await` other functions, use async iterators, whatever; it just has to make sure that it ultimately ends up using `time.sleep` instead of `trio.sleep` or `asyncio.sleep`, uses blocking I/O calls instead of trio/asyncio's I/O primitives, etc. Depending on how IPython is using these libraries, this might actually be pretty easy to do?

Technically speaking, it would be easy to support nested calls to `trio.run`. If you really need it then we could remove the check, or maybe add an I-know-what-I'm-doing option like `trio.run(..., allow_nested=True)` or something. But first I want to be sure you really do need it :-).

> Do you think it would be useful to add more wrappers for the non-base io classes?

I'd really rather not. Probably the simplest option would be to make the attribute that holds the wrapped object public, so in the rare cases where people need to access quirky attributes they can do `async_file_obj.wrapped.mode` or whatever?

> TextIOBase doesn't define `readinto`. Do you think it makes sense to do this anyway?

Ugh, good point. It might, since `readinto` is commonly available on random user-defined "file like" objects.

Well, here's an idea to consider: we could write the wrapper classes so that they only expose attributes that actually exist on the wrapped object. In Python, the only ways to tell whether a class has an attribute are (a) whether it shows up in `__dir__`, and (b) whether trying to access it raises `AttributeError`. (E.g. `hasattr` internally just tries to access the attribute, and returns `False` if it gets an `AttributeError`.) And notice that for the synchronous attributes, this PR's current `__getattr__`-based implementation will already raise `AttributeError` if the corresponding attribute is missing on the underlying object, so we're already half-way there. If we also used `__getattr__` for the async methods (i.e. generating the wrapper on each call to `__getattr__` instead of doing it at class initialization), and added a line like `attrs = [a for a in attrs if hasattr(self, a)]` to our `__dir__` method, then I think that would do it.

If going this way, it's even tempting to just have a single wrapper class for all "vaguely file-like objects" in Python, since as we've learned, the "file-like" interface is pretty vague to start with. That would also solve the problem with `pyfakefs`.

Hi @jkuehl2,

It took quite a while, but I have come up with a standalone test case that evokes the duplicate-message behavior. https://gist.github.com/AnvilStriker/b54c4d67b6c2a3e10b027553d0496fae

The test can be run in one of two "modes". With `GOPATH` set appropriately to resolve the package imports:
- `go test -run TestLocalstackKinesis -args -mode=func` gets duplicate messages
- `go test -run TestLocalstackKinesis -args -mode=chan` does not get duplicate messages
 
The difference seems to be in the rapidity with which successive calls to `GetRecords` are made when the test is run with `-mode=func`. In that mode, record-counting is done by a simple function called from within the loop that processes the `GetRecordsOutput`.

In the other mode, each received record is passed through a Go channel to a separate body of code that does the counting, and at least a couple of context-switches are needed before the next record is processed or next call to `GetRecords` is made.

I have considered doing it this way. Making it a separate method would mean that we would either have to keep it consistent with `query`'s input params or keep it simple and only support raw query strings, though. def decode_base64(data):
    """Decode base64, padding being optional.

    :param data: Base64 data as an ASCII byte string
    :returns: The decoded byte string.

    """
    missing_padding = len(data) % 4
    if missing_padding != 0:
        data += b'=' * (4 - missing_padding)
    return base64.decodestring(data)

On Nov 28, 2011, at 13:12, "Brian E. Granger"<reply@reply.github.com> wrote:

> I see two problems with the instance approach:
> 
> 1) if a user creates a loop using IOLoop(), which is very likely for simple usage cases, it won't work as expected.
> 

We don't have to monkey patch at all anymore. You can use regular tornado IOLoop objects with our ZMQPoller now, which is the official recommended approach from Tornado.  So the *only* thing we have a reason to patch is the instance singleton.

Note that *tornado* will not create multiple IOLoops, that's up to application developers, and if those developers want to use zmq sockets, they should be using the ZMQPoller or the pyzmq IOLoop (which is identical).

> 2) It becomes difficult to pass a specific zmq Context into the IOLoop, which happens quite often in complex apps.

I don't think this is relevant. The IOLoop and Context are perfectly orthogonal, and know nothing at all about each other, and never have.

> 
> Do you think the _instance approach can be made to handle these two cases?

the first case is handled *better* but slightly less easily, and I don't think the second case exists.
